<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Mintzberg's "Liaison" role and give one example from the HRM context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Liaison role is one of Mintzberg's three *Interpersonal roles*, where the manager builds and maintains a network of contacts both inside and outside the organization.
- **Example (1.5 marks):** For example, an HR Manager who coordinates between the **workers' union, the company's legal team, and senior management** during a wage revision exercise is performing the Liaison role.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Pattern:** KTU examiners expect the **definition + one applied example** structure for 3-mark questions. Skipping the example costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: List any three HR roles identified by Dave Ulrich.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Strategic Partner** (1 mark) – Aligns HR strategy with business strategy.
2. **Employee Champion** (1 mark) – Represents employee concerns to senior management.
3. **Change Agent** (1 mark) – Drives organizational transformation and manages the human side of change.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark "list any three" questions, write **only the role name + one-line function**. Adding elaborate descriptions wastes time without earning extra marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: Explain Henry Mintzberg's 10 managerial roles in detail. How are they classified into three broad categories? Illustrate with examples from a Human Resource Manager's context.**

#### Part (a) — Classification and Interpersonal/Informational Roles (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Mintzberg's 1973 study identified 10 distinct roles that all managers perform. These are grouped into 3 categories based on the *source* from which they arise.

**1. Interpersonal Roles** (arising from the manager's formal authority and status)
- **Figurehead:** Performs ceremonial duties. *HR Example:* The HR Manager inaugurating a new training facility.
- **Leader:** Motivates and guides subordinates. *HR Example:* Conducting a one-on-one performance review.
- **Liaison:** Builds networks inside and outside. *HR Example:* Coordinating with university placement cells for campus hiring.

**2. Informational Roles** (arising from the manager's access to information)
- **Monitor:** Scans the environment for relevant information. *HR Example:* Tracking changes in India's **Code on Wages, 2019**.
- **Disseminator:** Transmits information to subordinates. *HR Example:* Cascading a new leave policy from the CEO to all employees.
- **Spokesperson:** Represents the organization to outsiders. *HR Example:* Speaking at a press conference about the company's diversity hiring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often *list* the 10 roles without explaining the **source** (authority, information, or decision-making power) from which each category arises. KTU examiners explicitly award 1 mark for this "source" explanation. **Do not skip it.**

#### Part (b) — Decisional Roles and HR-Specific Application (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**3. Decisional Roles** (arising from the manager's authority to commit organizational resources)
- **Entrepreneur:** Initiates voluntary change. *HR Example:* Designing a new "flexi-work" policy.
- **Disturbance Handler:** Takes corrective action during crises. *HR Example:* Mediating a sudden walkout by manufacturing workers.
- **Resource Allocator:** Distributes resources. *HR Example:* Approving the annual L&D (Learning & Development) budget across departments.
- **Negotiator:** Represents the organization in major negotiations. *HR Example:* Bargaining with the trade union for a new collective agreement.

**Interconnection among the three categories:**
- The *Interpersonal* roles provide the manager with **information and access** (Liaison).
- This access is converted into *Informational* outputs (Monitor, Disseminator, Spokesperson).
- Finally, the manager uses the information to perform *Decisional* actions (Entrepreneur, etc.).

**Application to HRM:** In a modern organization, the HR Manager must *simultaneously* perform all 10 roles. For example, during a **merger or acquisition**, the HR Manager acts as a **Liaison** (with both firms' teams), a **Monitor** (of legal/regulatory changes), a **Spokesperson** (communicating with employees), an **Entrepreneur** (designing the integration plan), a **Resource Allocator** (deciding who stays/goes), and a **Negotiator** (settling employee terms).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Critically analyze Dave Ulrich's model of HR roles. Discuss the four original roles and explain how the addition of the 'Credible Activist' role in 2012 strengthened the model. Illustrate with an example from the Indian IT industry.**

#### Part (a) — The Four Original Roles (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Dave Ulrich's 1997 model redefined HR as a **strategic business partner** rather than a mere administrative function. The four original roles are:

1. **Strategic Partner** (1.5 marks)
   - Aligns HR strategy with business strategy.
   - Engages in workforce planning and succession planning.
   - *Indian IT example:* TCS's HR function forecasting hiring needs for its AI and cloud divisions.

2. **Administrative Expert** (1.5 marks)
   - Ensures efficient delivery of HR operational processes.
   - Manages payroll, compliance, and HRIS.
   - *Indian IT example:* Infosys's automated onboarding platform reducing time-to-join.

3. **Employee Champion** (1.5 marks)
   - Represents employee concerns to senior management.
   - Designs engagement and well-being programs.
   - *Indian IT example:* Wipro's "Spirit of Wipro" engagement program.

4. **Change Agent** (2.5 marks — *higher weightage as it is the most strategic*)
   - Manages organizational transformation.
   - Applies models like **Kotter's 8-Step Change Framework** or **ADKAR**.
   - *Indian IT example:* Leading the cultural integration during HCL Technologies' acquisition of Axon.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often describe all four roles with equal length. In a 7-mark sub-question, examiners allocate marks based on the *strategic weight* of each role. **Change Agent and Strategic Partner** typically carry more marks than Administrative Expert.

#### Part (b) — The 2012 Update and "Credible Activist" (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

In his 2012 book *"HR from the Outside In,"* Ulrich added a fifth role:

**5. Credible Activist (also called "Knowledge Worker")** (3 marks)
- An HR professional who combines **personal credibility** (being respected at the C-suite table) with **activism** (challenging the status quo using data and analytics).
- Uses **people analytics, HR dashboards, and predictive modeling** to drive evidence-based HR decisions.
- Acts as both a *strategic advisor* and a *data-driven challenger* of conventional thinking.

**Why the 2012 update strengthened the model:** (4 marks)
- The original 4-role model had a **gap**: it did not specify *how* the HR professional should personally behave to deliver on the strategic expectations.
- The 2012 update addressed this by demanding that HR professionals be **both credible (respected) and active (willing to challenge)**, with strong **business acumen and data fluency**.
- This transformed HR from a *function of policies* into a *function of measurable business impact*.

**Critical Evaluation:**
- *Strength:* Aligns HR with the era of **Big Data, AI, and People Analytics**.
- *Criticism:* Some scholars argue the five roles **overlap heavily** (e.g., Strategic Partner and Credible Activist both require business acumen), creating ambiguity in execution.
- *Indian IT relevance:* Companies like **Tata Consultancy Services** and **Infosys** have built in-house **People Analytics dashboards** that exemplify the Credible Activist role in action.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on "Roles of HR Managers" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Ulrich and Mintzberg:** Students often attribute *Ulrich's roles* to Mintzberg or vice-versa. This is an immediate **2-mark deduction** on a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Listing without classifying:** In a 14-mark question, simply listing 10 roles of Mintzberg without explaining the *three categories and their source* (authority / information / decision) leads to losing up to **3 marks**.
> 3. **No Indian context / examples:** KTU examiners consistently reward answers that cite **Indian laws (e.g., Industrial Disputes Act 1947)**, **Indian companies (e.g., TCS, Infosys)**, or **Indian industry examples**. Generic global examples may attract only partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Roles of HR Managers**

- ✅ **Mintzberg (1973)** = 10 roles in 3 categories: **Interpersonal (Figurehead, Leader, Liaison), Informational (Monitor, Disseminator, Spokesperson), Decisional (Entrepreneur, Disturbance Handler, Resource Allocator, Negotiator).**
- ✅ **Ulrich (1997)** = 4 original roles: **Strategic Partner, Administrative Expert, Employee Champion, Change Agent.**
- ✅ **Ulrich (2012)** = Added 5th role: **Credible Activist / Knowledge Worker.**
- ✅ **Source of roles** (KEY to scoring): Interpersonal → *formal authority*; Informational → *information access*; Decisional → *decision-making authority*.
- ✅ **Strategic Partner** = *Business alignment*; **Administrative Expert** = *Operational efficiency*; **Employee Champion** = *Engagement & welfare*; **Change Agent** = *Transformation*; **Credible Activist** = *Data-driven & influential*.
- ✅ **Inter-connection logic:** Interpersonal roles → Informational roles → Decisional roles (in that order, for both Mintzberg and Ulrich).
- ✅ **Indian legal anchors to remember:** **Industrial Disputes Act 1947** (grievance redressal), **Payment of Wages Act 1936** (payroll compliance), **EPF & MP Act 1952** (provident fund administration).
- ✅ **Modern tools to mention in answers:** **HRIS, People Analytics, HR Dashboards, Kotter's 8-Step Model, ADKAR, eNPS, Balanced Scorecard for HR.**
- ✅ **Memorize one Indian IT example** (TCS / Infosys / Wipro / HCL) for each role — this single habit can fetch an extra 2 marks in any 14-mark essay.
- ✅ **High-yield exam trigger words:** *"Illustrate with examples"* → always include a real-world case; *"Critically analyze"* → include both strengths and criticisms; *"Compare and contrast"* → use a comparison table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **(i) Theory (5 marks)** + **(ii) Classification/Framework explanation (4 marks)** + **(iii) Indian industrial example (3 marks)** + **(iv) Critical analysis or modern relevance (2 marks)**. This 5-4-3-2 split covers the entire mark distribution uniformly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to HR Analytics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to HR Analytics

# 📘 Introduction to HR Analytics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔹 Formal Academic Definition

**Human Resource (HR) Analytics** is the systematic application of statistical, mathematical, and computational methods to **People Data** in order to derive evidence-based insights for strategic workforce decision-making. It is formally defined as:

> *"HR Analytics is the methodology of collecting, measuring, and analyzing Human Capital data to understand, optimize, and predict employee performance, retention, recruitment efficiency, and overall organizational effectiveness."*

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (OEIET721 – Module 1), HR Analytics is positioned as the **data-driven backbone of modern HRM**, enabling the transition from *intuition-based decisions* to *evidence-based decisions*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The module introduces HR Analytics as a **strategic enabler**, covering definitions, evolution, types (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive), and its importance in modern workforce management.

### 🔹 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor treating a patient. In the **old days**, the doctor relied only on symptoms the patient described verbally. Today, doctors use **blood reports, X-rays, MRI scans** — measurable data — to make precise diagnoses and treatments.

**HR Analytics is the "MRI scan" of an organization's workforce.** Instead of HR managers *guessing* why employees leave, or *guessing* which candidate will perform best, HR Analytics uses **measurable HR data** (attrition %, time-to-hire, engagement scores, productivity per employee) to give precise, predictive answers.

| Old HR (Intuition-Based) | New HR (Analytics-Based) |
|---|---|
| "I feel our team is unhappy." | "Engagement score dropped **12%** in Q2." |
| "We should hire more people." | "Workload index shows we need **7** more engineers." |
| "Trainings are useful." | "Training ROI is **₹4.20 per ₹1 spent**." |

### 🔹 Core Standard Metrics in HR Analytics

The following **industry-standard KPIs** are explicitly emphasized in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Turnover Rate** — annual percentage of employees leaving
- **Time-to-Hire** — average days taken to fill a position
- **Cost-per-Hire** — total recruitment cost divided by number of hires
- **Absenteeism Rate** — unplanned absence as a percentage of working days
- **Employee Engagement Index** — composite score from surveys
- **Revenue per Employee** — organizational productivity metric
- **Training ROI** — benefit-to-cost ratio of learning programs

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** HR Analytics is **not** the same as HR Metrics. *Metrics* are simple measurements (e.g., 50 people left this year); *Analytics* interprets *why* they left, *predicts* who will leave next, and *prescribes* what to do about it.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔹 The "Why" and "How" of HR Analytics — Structured Logic

**Step 1 — Data Collection (The Foundation Layer)**
- Gather data from HRIS (Human Resource Information Systems), payroll, surveys, exit interviews, performance reviews.
- *Why?* Without quality data, analytics is meaningless ("Garbage In = Garbage Out").

**Step 2 — Data Cleaning & Integration**
- Remove duplicates, handle missing values, normalize formats.
- *How?* Tools like Excel, Power BI, Python (HR teams), or dedicated platforms like Workday, SAP SuccessFactors.

**Step 3 — Descriptive Analytics — "What Happened?"**
- Compute basic metrics: turnover %, average tenure, headcount by department.
- Uses: dashboards, summary statistics, trend charts.

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Analytics — "Why Did It Happen?"**
- Drill-down analysis, correlation studies, root-cause investigation.
- Example: Cross-tabulating turnover with manager-level, location, or tenure.

**Step 5 — Predictive Analytics — "What Will Happen?"**
- Apply regression models, classification algorithms, or survival analysis.
- Example: Predicting flight-risk employees based on engagement + tenure + compensation ratio.

**Step 6 — Prescriptive Analytics — "What Should We Do?"**
- Optimization models, scenario simulation.
- Example: Reallocate training budget to the department with the highest predicted attrition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Module 1 specifically highlights this **4-stage maturity model** (Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive). Examiners frequently ask students to *list and explain* these four types.

### 🔹 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Turnover Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Employees Left}}{\text{Average Headcount}} \times 100$ | **%** | Average Headcount = (Opening + Closing) / 2 |
| 2 | **Absenteeism Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Unplanned Absence Days}}{\text{Total Working Days}} \times 100$ | **%** | Excludes approved leave |
| 3 | **Time-to-Hire** | $\text{Date of Offer Accepted} - \text{Date Requisition Raised}$ | **Days** | Lower is better |
| 4 | **Cost-per-Hire** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Recruitment Cost}}{\text{Number of Hires}}$ | **₹ / $** | Internal + External costs |
| 5 | **Revenue per Employee** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Average Headcount}}$ | **₹ / $** | Higher indicates productivity |
| 6 | **Training ROI** | $\dfrac{\text{Training Benefit} - \text{Training Cost}}{\text{Training Cost}} \times 100$ | **%** | Benefit measured in productivity gain |
| 7 | **Engagement Score** | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Survey Ratings}}{\text{Number of Respondents}}$ | **0–5 scale** | Industry standard: Gallup Q12 |
| 8 | **Yield Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Number at Stage } n}{\text{Number at Stage } 1} \times 100$ | **%** | Tracks funnel efficiency |
| 9 | **HR-to-Employee Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{HR Staff}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100$ | **%** | Benchmark: 1.0–1.4% for large firms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Note carefully that Turnover Rate is computed using **Average Headcount**, not just opening headcount. This is a common examiner trap.

### 🔹 Real-World Utility of HR Analytics in Engineering Industries

In Kerala's IT, manufacturing, and emerging tech sectors (e.g., TCS Technopark, Infosys, Travancore Industries), HR Analytics is applied for:
- **Attrition prediction** in software teams
- **Skill-gap analysis** before project onboarding
- **Compensation benchmarking** against industry standards
- **Diversity & Inclusion tracking** (gender ratio, PwD inclusion)
- **Remote-work productivity** measurement (post-2020 WFH wave)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔹 Derivation 1 — Annual Turnover Rate from First Principles

The **Turnover Rate** is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation-style questions. Below is the full KTU-style derivation.

**Given:**
- Let $E_o$ = Number of employees at the **start** of the year (Opening headcount)
- Let $E_c$ = Number of employees at the **end** of the year (Closing headcount)
- Let $L$ = Number of employees who **left** during the year

**Step 1 — Conceptual Definition:**
Turnover is the *proportion* of the workforce that exited, normalized by the *average workforce size* during the year.

**Step 2 — Average Headcount Calculation:**
Since employees joined and left throughout the year, we cannot use just opening or closing count. We use the arithmetic mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{E_o + E_c}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Build the Ratio:**
The fraction of the workforce that turned over is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Turnover Fraction} &= \frac{L}{E_{\text{avg}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Turnover Rate (\%)} &= \frac{L}{E_{\text{avg}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{L}{\frac{E_o + E_c}{2}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2L}{E_o + E_c} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Generalized Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Turnover Rate (\%)} = \frac{2L}{E_o + E_c} \times 100\;}
$$

**Numerical Illustration:**
Suppose a Kochi-based IT firm had **E_o = 500**, **E_c = 540**, and **L = 60** leavers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Turnover Rate} &= \frac{2 \times 60}{500 + 540} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{120}{1040} \times 100 \\
&= 11.54\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 11.54% is a *healthy* turnover for the Indian IT industry (benchmark: 12–18%).

---

### 🔹 Derivation 2 — Training ROI (Phillips' Methodology)

The most academically accepted model is **Jack Phillips' Five-Level Evaluation Framework**, with Level 4 specifically measuring **ROI**.

**Step 1 — Convert performance improvement into monetary value.**

Let:
- $B$ = Annual monetary benefit (₹) from improved employee performance post-training
- $C$ = Total cost of training program (₹)
- $N$ = Number of trainees

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per-trainee Benefit} &= \frac{B}{N} \\
\text{Per-trainee Cost} &= \frac{C}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Net Benefit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= B - C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ROI Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI (\%)} &= \frac{B - C}{C} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCR} &= \frac{B}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Illustration:**
A Kerala-based manufacturing company invests **₹2,00,000** in a Six-Sigma training for **20** engineers. Post-training, productivity gains are estimated at **₹6,00,000/year**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCR} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For every ₹1 invested, ₹3 is returned — an **excellent** ROI. KTU examiners expect students to comment on *feasibility* and *practical applicability* of such returns.

---

### 🔹 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The 4 Types of HR Analytics

| Dimension | Descriptive | Diagnostic | Predictive | Prescriptive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Question** | What happened? | Why did it happen? | What will happen? | What should we do? |
| **Technique** | Reports, Dashboards | Drill-down, Correlation | Regression, ML | Optimization, Simulation |
| **Time Horizon** | Past | Past | Future | Future + Action |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Data Volume** | Small–Medium | Medium | Large | Very Large |
| **Business Value** | Basic Awareness | Root Cause Insight | Risk Forecasting | Strategic Optimization |
| **Example** | "12% left last year" | "They left due to manager + low pay" | "X will leave in 6 months" | "Adjust pay + train managers" |
| **Tools** | Excel, HRIS | Power BI, Tableau | Python, R, SPSS | Gurobi, AnyLogic |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define HR Analytics. List any four HR metrics used in workforce analytics.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
HR Analytics is the systematic application of statistical and data-driven methods to measure, analyze, and optimize human capital outcomes in an organization. It transforms raw HR data into actionable business insights.

Four HR metrics are:
1. **Turnover Rate** — % of employees leaving
2. **Time-to-Hire** — days to fill a vacancy
3. **Cost-per-Hire** — recruitment cost per new employee
4. **Employee Engagement Score** — survey-based satisfaction index

---

**Q2. Differentiate between HR Metrics and HR Analytics.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HR Metrics | HR Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Quantitative measurement | Interpretive + Predictive science |
| Question Answered | "What?" | "Why?" and "What next?" |
| Depth | Descriptive surface-level | Diagnostic + Predictive depth |
| Example | "50 people left" | "Senior engineers in Project-X dept with salary below market median are 3× more likely to leave" |
| Tools | Excel, HRIS | Python, R, Power BI, ML models |

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **four types of HR Analytics** — Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive — with a suitable example for each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Bangalore-based engineering firm had the following data for 2023:
- Opening Headcount: **800**
- Closing Headcount: **920**
- Number of Leavers: **144**
- Recruitment Cost: **₹18,00,000**
- Number of Hires: **120**

**Calculate**:
(i) Annual Turnover Rate
(ii) Cost-per-Hire

Interpret the results from an HR Analytics perspective. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Solution A:**

**(a) Four Types of HR Analytics:**

1. **Descriptive Analytics — "What Happened?"**
   - Uses dashboards, summary reports, and trend charts to summarize historical HR data.
   - *Example:* Last quarter's attrition was **11%**, dominated by junior developers.

2. **Diagnostic Analytics — "Why Did It Happen?"**
   - Uses drill-down, correlation, and root-cause analysis.
   - *Example:* Exit-interview + payroll data reveals **78% of leavers cited poor manager relationship**.

3. **Predictive Analytics — "What Will Happen?"**
   - Uses regression, classification, and survival models on historical data.
   - *Example:* Logistic regression predicts **23 employees in Team-A** are at high flight-risk in the next 6 months.

4. **Prescriptive Analytics — "What Should We Do?"**
   - Uses optimization and simulation to recommend actions.
   - *Example:* Allocate the **₹10 lakh training budget to Team-A managers** to reduce attrition risk by an estimated **40%**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Annual Turnover Rate:**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Turnover Rate} &= \frac{2L}{E_o + E_c} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2 \times 144}{800 + 920} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{288}{1720} \times 100 \\
&= 16.74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cost-per-Hire:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost-per-Hire} &= \frac{\text{Total Recruitment Cost}}{\text{Number of Hires}} \\
&= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{120} \\
&= ₹15{,}000 \text{ per hire}
\end{aligned}
$$

**HR Analytics Interpretation:**
- A **16.74%** turnover is **above the Indian IT industry benchmark (12–18%)** — concerning, requires intervention.
- A **₹15,000** cost-per-hire is **within industry range** (₹10,000–₹25,000) — efficient.
- **Recommendation:** Launch a predictive analytics model for flight-risk identification and invest in manager-training programs to reduce attrition.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **evolution of HR Analytics** from traditional HR reporting to modern data-driven decision-making. Highlight the role of technology in this transformation. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A company invests **₹5,00,000** in a leadership training program for **25** managers. Post-training, the company estimates a productivity gain worth **₹15,00,000** annually.

**Calculate**:
(i) Training ROI (%)
(ii) Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

Comment on whether the training program should be continued. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **Model Solution B:**

**(a) Evolution of HR Analytics:**

| Era | Time Period | Focus | Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| Era 1: Traditional HR | 1950s–1990s | Personnel records, payroll | Paper files, basic software |
| Era 2: HRIS Era | 1990s–2010s | Automated record-keeping | SAP HR, Oracle HRMS |
| Era 3: HR Metrics | 2000s–2015 | Reporting dashboards | Excel, MIS reports |
| Era 4: HR Analytics | 2015–Present | Predictive + Prescriptive insights | Power BI, Python, ML |
| Era 5: People Analytics 4.0 | 2020–Future | Real-time AI-driven decisions | AI Chatbots, NLP, Gen-AI |

The shift is powered by **cloud computing, big data, machine learning, and IoT-enabled workforce sensors**. Modern HR Analytics combines **internal HR data** with **external labour-market data** for holistic decision-making.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Training ROI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI (\%)} &= \frac{B - C}{C} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCR} &= \frac{B}{C} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:**
- ROI of **200%** is exceptional (industry benchmark: >50%).
- BCR of **3.0** means every ₹1 spent returns ₹3.
- **Recommendation:** **Yes**, the program must be continued and scaled to other departments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using opening headcount alone** instead of average — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** in percentage formulas — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not interpreting the answer** — KTU awards 2 marks for the analytical interpretation; merely stating numbers gets 0 for interpretation.
> 4. **Confusing Turnover with Attrition** — technically interchangeable in exam contexts, but stick with the term used in the question.
> 5. **Skipping the 4-type analytics listing** — Module 1's most-missed question; examiners expect all four with examples.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **HR Analytics = Data-driven HR** — uses statistical/mathematical tools on People Data.
- ✅ **4 Types (DDPP)**: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- ✅ **Turnover Rate Formula**: $\dfrac{2L}{E_o + E_c} \times 100$ — always use **average headcount**.
- ✅ **Cost-per-Hire** = Recruitment Cost ÷ Number of Hires.
- ✅ **Training ROI** = $\dfrac{B - C}{C} \times 100$ — **Phillips' methodology** is the academic standard.
- ✅ **HR Metrics ≠ HR Analytics** — Metrics = measurements; Analytics = interpretation + prediction.
- ✅ **Maturity Pyramid**: Data → Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- ✅ **Industry tools**: Excel, Power BI, Tableau, Python, R, SAP SuccessFactors, Workday.
- ✅ **Application sectors**: IT, manufacturing, BPO, healthcare, BFSI — everywhere humans work.
- ✅ **KTU exam weightage**: 2–3 mark questions on definitions; 14-mark derivations on Turnover/ROI are **guaranteed** every semester.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HR Accounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HR Accounting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Human Resource Accounting (HRA)** is the process of identifying, quantifying, measuring, classifying, and reporting an organization's investment in its human resources and the economic value of people as organizational assets in monetary terms.

As defined by the **American Accounting Association (1973)**, HRA is the process of identifying, measuring, and communicating information about human resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> HR Accounting is a branch of accounting that treats human beings as organizational resources (assets) and measures the cost incurred in recruiting, selecting, hiring, training, and developing employees, along with the economic value they generate for the firm.

The **core premise** is that people are *valuable resources* like any other asset (machinery, buildings, patents) and therefore their cost and value should appear on the **Balance Sheet** of the organization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a manufacturing firm that owns a **₹1 Crore CNC machine**. The firm records this in its balance sheet as an asset, depreciates it over time, and shows its book value.

Now consider **Mr. Sharma**, a senior design engineer with 15 years of domain expertise, who generates ₹50 Lakhs in innovation revenue every year. *Logically, is he not a more valuable asset than the machine?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Problem HRA Solves**
> Traditional balance sheets completely ignore human capital. HRA attempts to correct this by giving a **rupee value** to people — turning "employees" from an expense item into a **measurable strategic asset**.

This shift in mindset allows management to:
- Justify investments in training and development
- Quantify the cost of employee turnover
- Track the ROI of human capital programs

### 1.3 Key Terminology in HRA

| Term | Meaning | KTU 2024 Relevance |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Human Capital** | The economic value of an employee's knowledge, skills, and experience | Central to all HRA methods |
| **Human Asset Valuation** | Process of assigning monetary value to human resources | Core step in HRA |
| **HR Cost** | Money spent on recruitment, training, compensation | Input side of HRA |
| **HR Value** | Economic worth an employee generates | Output side of HRA |
| **Discount Rate (r)** | Rate used to convert future earnings into present value | Used in Lev & Schwartz Model |

### 1.4 Pioneers of HR Accounting (Board-Favorite)

- **Rensis Likert** (1967) — Proposed the *Human Asset Model* valuing employees at replacement cost.
- **Eric Flamholtz** (1968, 1974) — Developed the *Stochastic Rewards Valuation Model*.
- **Lev & Schwartz** (1971) — Pioneered the *Present Value of Future Earnings Model*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Board Fact:** Always attribute the *Present Value of Future Earnings* method to **Lev and Schwartz (1971)** in KTU answers for maximum marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives / Need for HR Accounting

1. **Decision-Making:** Helps top management make informed choices on hiring, transfers, and layoffs.
2. **Performance Evaluation:** Enables comparison of the *value* added by different employees.
3. **Cost Reduction:** Identifies areas of wasteful HR expenditure.
4. **Capital Investment Justification:** Validates the ROI of training programs.
5. **Disclosure Obligation:** Provides transparent information to investors, shareholders, and stakeholders.
6. **Talent Retention:** Highlights the financial cost of *employee turnover*.
7. **Strategic Planning:** Aligns workforce planning with long-term business strategy.

### 2.2 Methods/Models of HR Accounting (Detailed)

#### 2.2.1 Historical Cost Method
- **Logic:** Records all historical expenditure incurred on acquiring and developing an employee.
- **Includes:** Recruitment cost, selection cost, training cost, development cost, compensation.
- **Formula:**
$$HC = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (C_{rt} + C_{st} + C_{tt} + C_{dt})$$

Where:
- $HC$ = Historical cost of human asset
- $C_{rt}$ = Recruitment cost in year t
- $C_{st}$ = Selection cost
- $C_{tt}$ = Training cost
- $C_{dt}$ = Development cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Simple, objective, based on verifiable accounting data. But it ignores *future value* of the employee.

#### 2.2.2 Replacement Cost Method
- **Logic:** Estimates the cost a firm would have to incur to *replace* an existing employee with a new one of equal capability.
- **Two Variants:**
  - **Individual Replacement Cost** — Cost to replace one specific employee.
  - **Organizational Replacement Cost** — Cost to replace the entire workforce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** This method is preferred by **Rensis Likert** for practical valuation.

#### 2.2.3 Opportunity Cost Method
- **Logic:** Based on the economic principle of *opportunity cost*. HR value is the maximum price a competing firm is willing to pay to hire that employee.
- **Mechanism:** In a perfectly competitive market, if two firms bid for an employee, the value is determined by the *bid price*.
- **Limitation:** Subjective and difficult to implement in non-bidding scenarios.

#### 2.2.4 Standard Cost Method
- **Logic:** Sets *standard costs* for various HR functions (recruitment, training, etc.) based on industry benchmarks.
- **Formula:**
$$HC_{standard} = \sum (C_{standard} \times Q_{actual})$$

#### 2.2.5 Current Purchasing Power (CPP) Method
- **Logic:** Adjusts the historical cost of human assets for *inflation* to reflect real value in current price levels.
- **Formula:**
$$HC_{CPP} = HC_{historical} \times \frac{CPI_{current}}{CPI_{base}}$$

#### 2.2.6 Present Value of Future Earnings Method (Lev & Schwartz Model) ⭐

This is the most important method for KTU numerical problems.

$$V_{y} = \sum_{t=y}^{T} \frac{I_{t}}{(1+r)^{t-y}} \times P(y, t)$$

Where:
- $V_{y}$ = Present value of human capital of a person of age y
- $I_{t}$ = Expected average earnings of the person in year t
- $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital)
- $P(y, t)$ = Probability that the person survives in service from age y to age t
- $T$ = Retirement age

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Key Formula | When Used | Board Marks Weight |
|--------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Historical Cost | $HC = \sum C_{t}$ | Easy subjective questions | 2 marks |
| Replacement Cost | $RC =$ Cost of substitution | Likert-type questions | 3 marks |
| Opportunity Cost | Highest bid price | Conceptual Qs only | 2 marks |
| Lev & Schwartz | $V_{y} = \sum \frac{I_{t}}{(1+r)^{t-y}} P(y,t)$ | **Numerical problems (14 marks)** | ⭐ High |
| CPP Method | $HC_{CPP} = HC_{hist} \times \frac{CPI_{curr}}{CPI_{base}}$ | Inflation-adjusted valuation | 2 marks |
| Flamholtz | Stochastic rewards × probability | Theory questions | 3 marks |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Industry

- **Pharma & IT Sectors:** Heavy reliance on skilled researchers/developers makes HRA critical for M&A valuations.
- **Consulting Firms:** Use HRA to defend billing rates (the human is the *product*).
- **Stock Market:** Emerging frameworks like **HCM (Human Capital Management) Metrics** by ISO 30414 are standardizing HR disclosure.
- **Banks & Financial Institutions:** Use HRA to assess the *intangible asset* value of a target company during acquisitions.

### 2.5 Advantages of HR Accounting

1. Provides a **realistic view** of the organization's true value.
2. Encourages **scientific HR decision-making**.
3. Helps in **cost-benefit analysis** of HR programs.
4. Improves **internal reporting** quality.
5. Strengthens the **employer brand** by showing commitment to human capital.

### 2.6 Limitations of HR Accounting

1. **Heterogeneity of people** — every individual is unique; no two employees can be valued identically.
2. **No universal standard** — different methods give different valuations.
3. **Subjective assumptions** — future earnings and survival probabilities are estimates.
4. **Non-monetary benefits** — loyalty, culture, motivation are hard to quantify.
5. **Resistance from management** — viewing people as "assets" may dehumanize the workforce.
6. **Ethical concerns** — risk of discrimination based on calculated value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Logic Behind the Lev & Schwartz Formula

The Lev & Schwartz model rests on **three core economic principles**:

**Step 1:** Treat human capital as a stream of future income.
- An employee is valuable because of the *future economic benefits* they will generate.
- This is analogous to a financial bond — a stream of future coupons.

**Step 2:** Discount the future stream to present value.
- A rupee earned in 2030 is worth less than a rupee earned today.
- The discount factor is $\frac{1}{(1+r)^{t-y}}$.

**Step 3:** Adjust for attrition/uncertainty.
- Employees may leave, retire, or pass away. Multiply the discounted earnings by the *probability of survival in service* $P(y, t)$.

**Step 4:** Sum across all years from current age $y$ to retirement age $T$.

**Generalised Form:**
$$V_{y} = \sum_{t=y}^{T} \frac{I_{t}}{(1+r)^{t-y}} \times P(y, t)$$

### 3.2 Numerical Problem (Model Answer Style)

**Problem Statement:**
> Compute the human capital value of **Mr. Karthik**, age 25, working at Zest Technologies Pvt. Ltd. His expected average annual earnings are:
> - Age 25–30: ₹3,00,000 per annum
> - Age 30–35: ₹5,00,000 per annum
> - Age 35–40: ₹7,00,000 per annum
>
> Probability of survival in service:
> - Up to age 30: 0.9
> - Up to age 35: 0.8
> - Up to age 40: 0.6
>
> Discount rate = **10%** per annum. Retirement age = 40.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the three earning phases and corresponding survival probabilities.

- **Phase 1 (Years 1–5):** Earnings = ₹3,00,000; $P_1$ = 0.9
- **Phase 2 (Years 6–10):** Earnings = ₹5,00,000; $P_2$ = 0.8
- **Phase 3 (Years 11–15):** Earnings = ₹7,00,000; $P_3$ = 0.6

**Step 2:** Compute the present value annuity factors.

**Phase 1 (Years 1 to 5):**

The present value of an annuity of ₹1 for 5 years at 10% is:

$$PVIFA(10\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.1)^{-5}}{0.1} = \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.1} = 3.7908$$

**Phase 2 (Years 6 to 10):**

This is the annuity factor for 10 years minus the annuity factor for 5 years:

$$PVIFA(10\%, 6\text{ to }10) = PVIFA(10\%, 10) - PVIFA(10\%, 5)$$

$$= 6.1446 - 3.7908 = 2.3538$$

**Phase 3 (Years 11 to 15):**

$$PVIFA(10\%, 11\text{ to }15) = PVIFA(10\%, 15) - PVIFA(10\%, 10)$$

$$= 7.6061 - 6.1446 = 1.4615$$

**Step 3:** Multiply each phase's earnings by the survival probability and annuity factor.

**Phase 1 Valuation:**

$$V_{1} = 3,00,000 \times 0.9 \times 3.7908$$

$$V_{1} = 3,00,000 \times 3.4117 = 10,23,510$$

**Phase 2 Valuation:**

$$V_{2} = 5,00,000 \times 0.8 \times 2.3538$$

$$V_{2} = 5,00,000 \times 1.8830 = 9,41,520$$

**Phase 3 Valuation:**

$$V_{3} = 7,00,000 \times 0.6 \times 1.4615$$

$$V_{3} = 7,00,000 \times 0.8769 = 6,13,830$$

**Step 4:** Sum all phases to get the total human capital value.

$$V_{Total} = V_{1} + V_{2} + V_{3}$$

$$V_{Total} = 10,23,510 + 9,41,520 + 6,13,830$$

$$\boxed{V_{Total} = \text{₹}25,78,860}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

The human capital value of Mr. Karthik, aged 25, is **₹25,78,860** in present-value terms. This represents the *economic value* the organization can expect from him during his service period.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods

| Criterion | Historical Cost | Replacement Cost | Opportunity Cost | Lev & Schwartz |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Ease of Application** | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Data Requirement** | Low (existing records) | Medium | High (bidding data) | High (actuarial data) |
| **Future-Oriented** | No (backward looking) | Partially | Yes | Yes (strongly) |
| **Subjectivity** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Theoretical Soundness** | Weak | Medium | Strong (microecon) | Strong (finance theory) |
| **Practical Use in India** | Limited | Limited | Rare | Common in research |
| **KTU 2024 Board Frequency** | Medium | High | Medium | **Very High** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HR Accounting Conceptual Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Human Resource Accounting System] --> B0[Input Side: HR Costs]
    A0 --> C0[Output Side: HR Value]

    B0 --> B1[Recruitment Cost]
    B0 --> B2[Selection Cost]
    B0 --> B3[Training Cost]
    B0 --> B4[Development Cost]
    B0 --> B5[Compensation Cost]

    C0 --> C1[Individual Value]
    C0 --> C2[Group Value]
    C0 --> C3[Organizational Value]

    B1 --> D0[Valuation Engine]
    B2 --> D0
    B3 --> D0
    B4 --> D0
    B5 --> D0

    D0 --> E1[Historical Cost Method]
    D0 --> E2[Replacement Cost Method]
    D0 --> E3[Opportunity Cost Method]
    D0 --> E4[Lev and Schwartz Model]

    E1 --> F0[Financial Statements and Reports]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    E4 --> F0

    C1 --> F0
    C2 --> F0
    C3 --> F0

    F0 --> G0[Strategic HR Decision Making]
    F0 --> G1[Investor Disclosure]
    F0 --> G2[Talent Retention Strategy]
```

### 4.2 Lev & Schwartz Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I0[Employee Profile Input] --> I1[Current Age Y]
    I0 --> I2[Retirement Age T]
    I0 --> I3[Expected Earnings I sub t]
    I0 --> I4[Discount Rate R]
    I0 --> I5[Survival Probability P Y T]

    P1[Phase 1 Earnings Block] --> P1A[Multiply by survival probability]
    P1A --> P1B[Apply discount factor 1 over 1 plus r power t minus y]
    P1B --> P1C[Sum across years 1 to 5]

    P2[Phase 2 Earnings Block] --> P2A[Multiply by survival probability]
    P2A --> P2B[Apply discount factor 1 over 1 plus r power t minus y]
    P2B --> P2C[Sum across years 6 to 10]

    P3[Phase 3 Earnings Block] --> P3A[Multiply by survival probability]
    P3A --> P3B[Apply discount factor 1 over 1 plus r power t minus y]
    P3B --> P3C[Sum across years 11 to 15]

    P1C --> SUM[Aggregator Block]
    P2C --> SUM
    P3C --> SUM

    SUM --> OUT[Final Human Capital Value V sub y]
    OUT --> R1[Balance Sheet Disclosure]
    OUT --> R2[HR Strategy Formulation]
    OUT --> R3[Investor Reporting]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting an HRA Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose HRA Method] --> q1{Is data on past costs available?}

    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is future income stream predictable?}
    q1 -- No --> m1[Use Replacement Cost Method]

    q2 -- Yes --> m2[Use Lev and Schwartz Model]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is a competitive bidding market available?}

    q3 -- Yes --> m3[Use Opportunity Cost Method]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Need inflation adjustment?}

    q4 -- Yes --> m4[Use CPP Method]
    q4 -- No --> m5[Use Standard Cost Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The Lev & Schwartz block diagram above is the most-tested architecture. Examiners often ask students to *draw or explain the flow* from inputs (employee profile) to outputs (final valuation).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Human Resource Accounting. List any two of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Human Resource Accounting is the process of identifying, measuring, and reporting the cost and value of human resources of an organization in monetary terms. It treats employees as valuable organizational assets whose economic contribution should be quantified and disclosed.

**Objectives (any two):**
1. To provide a true and fair view of the organization's total resources by including human assets.
2. To assist management in making effective and scientific decisions on recruitment, training, and retention of employees.
3. To enable cost-benefit analysis of human resource development programs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Briefly explain the Historical Cost Method of Human Resource Accounting.**

**Model Answer:**
The Historical Cost Method was one of the earliest approaches to HRA. Under this method, all costs incurred by an organization in acquiring, recruiting, selecting, training, and developing an employee are recorded as the *historical cost* of that human asset. These costs are capitalized (i.e., treated as an investment) and shown as an asset in the balance sheet. Over the years, this cost is amortized in a manner similar to the depreciation of physical assets. The method is simple, objective, and based on verifiable accounting data, but it fails to capture the *future economic value* generated by the employee.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 1 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) carry **14 marks** and offer an *internal choice* between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different methods of Human Resource Accounting. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Human Resource Accounting can be performed using several methods, each with a distinct theoretical basis:

1. **Historical Cost Method:** Capitalizes the actual past expenditure on recruitment, selection, training, and development of employees. It is objective and easy to apply but ignores future earning potential.

2. **Replacement Cost Method:** Estimates the cost the firm would incur to *replace* an existing employee with another of equivalent capability. It has two variants: *Individual Replacement Cost* and *Organizational Replacement Cost*. Pioneered by Rensis Likert, it reflects the *current* value of human assets.

3. **Opportunity Cost Method:** Values employees at the maximum price a competing firm is willing to pay in a competitive bidding scenario. While theoretically sound, it is rarely applied in practice due to lack of bidding data.

4. **Standard Cost Method:** Assigns pre-determined standard costs to HR activities based on industry benchmarks. The standard cost is multiplied by the actual quantity of HR services to derive total HR cost.

5. **Current Purchasing Power (CPP) Method:** Adjusts the historical cost of human assets using a price index (CPI) to express them in current monetary terms, thereby accounting for inflation.

6. **Present Value of Future Earnings (Lev & Schwartz) Method:** Estimates the present value of all future expected earnings of an employee, discounted at a suitable rate and adjusted for the probability of survival in service. This is the most rigorous method and is widely tested in KTU examinations.

**(b)** Discuss the limitations of Human Resource Accounting. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Despite its strategic value, HRA suffers from several practical and theoretical limitations:

1. **Heterogeneity of Human Beings:** Unlike machines, no two employees are identical. Valuing them using a single monetary yardstick is inherently reductionist.

2. **Lack of Universally Accepted Standards:** Different methods produce vastly different valuations, creating confusion. No GAAP or IFRS equivalent exists for HRA reporting.

3. **Subjectivity in Assumptions:** Future earnings, survival probabilities, and discount rates are all *estimates*, introducing significant bias.

4. **Ethical Concerns:** Valuing humans in monetary terms may lead to discriminatory practices such as layoffs of "low-value" employees, raising serious ethical issues.

5. **Difficulty in Quantifying Intangibles:** Loyalty, motivation, organizational culture, and team synergy are difficult to translate into financial figures.

6. **Management Resistance:** Many organizations view HRA as a theoretical exercise with limited operational utility, leading to poor adoption.

7. **No Recognition in Statutory Accounts:** Indian Companies Act, 2013 does not mandate HRA disclosure, making it optional and inconsistently reported.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **Lev & Schwartz Model** of Human Capital Valuation with its formula. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Lev & Schwartz Model (1971) is the most academically rigorous and frequently examined method of HR Accounting. It conceptualizes human capital as the *present value of an individual's future expected earnings*, discounted at an appropriate rate, and adjusted for the probability that the person will remain in service.

**Formula:**

$$V_{y} = \sum_{t=y}^{T} \frac{I_{t}}{(1+r)^{t-y}} \times P(y, t)$$

**Explanation of Variables:**

- $V_{y}$ = Present value of human capital of a person currently aged $y$.
- $I_{t}$ = Expected average annual earnings of the person during year $t$.
- $r$ = Discount rate (organization's cost of capital).
- $t - y$ = Number of years from current age $y$ to year $t$.
- $P(y, t)$ = Probability that the person will survive and remain in service from age $y$ to age $t$.
- $T$ = Retirement age.

**Procedure:**

1. Estimate the future earnings of the person in each year until retirement.
2. Multiply each year's earnings by the *probability of survival in service* to account for attrition.
3. Discount each value to the present using the factor $\frac{1}{(1+r)^{t-y}}$.
4. Sum up all the discounted values to obtain the total human capital value.

This model is theoretically sound because it follows the same discounting logic used in finance to value bonds and other financial assets.

**(b)** The expected average annual earnings of Mr. Arun, age 28, are as follows:

- Age 28–33: ₹4,00,000 per year
- Age 33–38: ₹6,00,000 per year
- Age 38–43: ₹8,00,000 per year

Probability of survival in service:
- Up to age 33: 0.90
- Up to age 38: 0.75
- Up to age 43: 0.55

Discount rate = **12%** per annum. Retirement age = 43.

**Compute the human capital value of Mr. Arun using the Lev & Schwartz Model.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify the three phases**

- Phase 1 (Years 1–5): Earnings = ₹4,00,000, $P_1$ = 0.90
- Phase 2 (Years 6–10): Earnings = ₹6,00,000, $P_2$ = 0.75
- Phase 3 (Years 11–15): Earnings = ₹8,00,000, $P_3$ = 0.55

**Step 2: Calculate the annuity factors at 12% discount rate**

Annuity factor for 5 years at 12%:

$$PVIFA(12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

Annuity factor for 10 years at 12%:

$$PVIFA(12\%, 10) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-10}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.3220}{0.12} = 5.6502$$

Annuity factor for 15 years at 12%:

$$PVIFA(12\%, 15) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-15}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.1827}{0.12} = 6.8109$$

**Step 3: Calculate annuity factors for specific phases**

Phase 1 (Years 1 to 5) = 3.6048

Phase 2 (Years 6 to 10):

$$= PVIFA(12\%, 10) - PVIFA(12\%, 5) = 5.6502 - 3.6048 = 2.0454$$

Phase 3 (Years 11 to 15):

$$= PVIFA(12\%, 15) - PVIFA(12\%, 10) = 6.8109 - 5.6502 = 1.1607$$

**Step 4: Calculate the present value of each phase**

Phase 1:

$$V_{1} = 4,00,000 \times 0.90 \times 3.6048 = 12,97,728$$

Phase 2:

$$V_{2} = 6,00,000 \times 0.75 \times 2.0454 = 9,20,430$$

Phase 3:

$$V_{3} = 8,00,000 \times 0.55 \times 1.1607 = 5,10,708$$

**Step 5: Sum all phases to get the total human capital value**

$$V_{Total} = V_{1} + V_{2} + V_{3}$$

$$V_{Total} = 12,97,728 + 9,20,430 + 5,10,708$$

$$\boxed{V_{Total} = \text{₹}27,28,866}$$

**Interpretation:**
The human capital value of Mr. Arun, aged 28, is **₹27,28,866** in present value terms. This represents the economic value Zest Technologies can expect to derive from him over his remaining service tenure, considering attrition and the time value of money.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $P(y, t)$:** The most common mistake. Always adjust each phase by the survival probability. *(−3 marks)*
> 2. **Using a single annuity factor for all phases:** Each phase has its own annuity factor (1–5, 6–10, 11–15). Don't apply $PVIFA(15\%, r)$ to the entire stream. *(−4 marks)*
> 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** Always mention discount rate, retirement age, and that survival probabilities are based on industry actuarial data. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step:** Examiners award a full mark for clearly stating what the final figure represents.
> 5. **Confusing replacement cost with opportunity cost:** Replacement cost = cost to substitute the employee. Opportunity cost = highest bid price in a competitive market. *They are not the same.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HR Accounting**

- **Definition:** HRA = identification, measurement, and reporting of human resource cost and value in monetary terms.
- **Pioneers:**
  - **Rensis Likert** → Human Asset Model
  - **Flamholtz** → Stochastic Rewards Model
  - **Lev & Schwartz** → Present Value of Future Earnings
- **Key Methods (6):** Historical Cost, Replacement Cost, Opportunity Cost, Standard Cost, CPP, Lev & Schwartz.
- **Master Formula (Lev & Schwartz):**

$$V_{y} = \sum_{t=y}^{T} \frac{I_{t}}{(1+r)^{t-y}} \times P(y, t)$$

- **Numerical Strategy for KTU:**
  1. Identify phases and survival probabilities.
  2. Compute annuity factors for each phase.
  3. Multiply: Earnings × Probability × Annuity Factor.
  4. Sum all phases → Total Human Capital Value.
- **Advantages:** Better decision-making, ROI justification, transparent reporting.
- **Limitations:** Heterogeneity, lack of standards, ethical concerns, no statutory mandate.
- **Indian Context:** The **Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI)** has not made HRA disclosure mandatory under the Companies Act, 2013 — a key limitation in Indian practice.
- **Real-World Application:** Most relevant in knowledge-intensive industries (IT, Pharma, Consulting, BFSI).
- **Mnemonic for Methods:** "**HRS-OCL**" → Historical, Replacement, Standard, Opportunity, CPP, Lev & Schwartz.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — HR Accounting**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HR Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HR Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **HR AUDIT (Formal Definition)**
> An **HR Audit** is a comprehensive, systematic, and methodical evaluation of an organization's human resource policies, practices, procedures, systems, strategies, and overall HR functioning, conducted to verify alignment with **statutory compliance**, **organizational objectives**, and **industry best practices**, while identifying areas for improvement, efficiency enhancement, and strategic value addition.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEIET721 – Human Resource Management)**, an HR Audit is positioned as a **strategic diagnostic tool** that measures the degree to which the HR function contributes to organizational effectiveness and competitive advantage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> Students must understand the *concept, objectives, scope, process, models, benefits, and limitations* of HR Auditing. Questions frequently appear in the form of **2-mark short definitions** and **14-mark descriptive essays** on the audit process and benchmarking.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The HR Medical Check-up"

Imagine the **Human Resource Department** as the **heart** of an organization — it pumps talent, culture, compliance, and performance throughout the company. Just as a person goes for an **annual medical check-up** to detect early signs of illness (high BP, cholesterol, diabetes) before they become fatal, an **HR Audit** is the equivalent of an **annual health check-up for the HR function**.

| Medical Check-up | HR Audit |
|---|---|
| Blood test & ECG | Review of HR policies & procedures |
| Cholesterol report | Employee satisfaction & engagement data |
| Doctor's diagnosis | Auditor's findings & gap analysis |
| Prescription | Recommendations & action plan |
| Follow-up visits | Continuous monitoring & re-audit |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hack for KTU Exam:** Think **HEART** —
> **H** – Health check of HR function
> **E** – Evaluate compliance
> **A** – Assess effectiveness
> **R** – Recommend improvements
> **T** – Track implementation

---

## 1.3 Standard HR Audit Metrics (Industry-Standard)

- **HR-to-Employee Ratio:** Typically **1:100** in mature organizations.
- **HR Cost as % of Total Operating Cost:** Benchmark range of **3% to 6%**.
- **Employee Turnover Rate:** Healthy range **10%–15%** annually.
- **Training Investment per Employee:** Benchmark of **$1,500 per employee/year** (industry varies).
- **Time-to-Fill Vacancy:** Standard of **30–45 days**.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are mentioned in bold as standard industry benchmarks that students may cite in 14-mark KTU answers to demonstrate applied knowledge.

---

## 1.4 Evolution and Genesis of HR Audit

| Era | Focus | Auditing Style |
|---|---|---|
| 1920s–1950s | Personnel records & payroll accuracy | **Clerical / Compliance Audit** |
| 1960s–1980s | Employee relations & grievance redressal | **Compliance + Legal Audit** |
| 1990s | Strategic HRM, TQM, ISO standards | **Strategic / Performance Audit** |
| 2000s–Present | Talent analytics, HR transformation | **Strategic & Technology-Driven Audit** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked *"Trace the evolution of HR Audit,"* always begin with the **clerk-comparison era** (when HR was just record-keeping) and conclude with the **strategic era** (HR as a business partner). This shows the examiner a *cause-and-effect narrative* — a hallmark of a **14-mark answer**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Objectives of an HR Audit

The **objectives** form the conceptual backbone of any KTU exam question on HR Audit. The audit is performed to achieve the following seven (7) high-priority objectives:

1. **Compliance Verification** — Ensuring adherence to labour laws, employment acts, and statutory requirements.
2. **Policy Effectiveness Review** — Assessing whether existing HR policies are relevant, current, and being followed.
3. **Performance Measurement** — Evaluating the contribution of HR to organizational KPIs.
4. **Gap Identification** — Detecting mismatches between *current state* and *desired state* of HR practices.
5. **Cost Optimization** — Identifying wasteful HR expenditures and re-allocating resources efficiently.
6. **Risk Mitigation** — Proactively identifying legal, financial, and reputational HR risks.
7. **Strategic Alignment** — Ensuring HR strategy aligns with overall business strategy.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "C-P-P-G-C-R-S":**
> **C**ompliance → **P**olicy → **P**erformance → **G**ap → **C**ost → **R**isk → **S**trategy

---

## 2.2 Types / Models of HR Audit

There are **seven widely recognized models** of HR Audit. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes at least the first four.

| S.No | Type / Model | Core Focus | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Compliance Audit** | Legal & statutory adherence | Annual mandatory checks |
| 2 | **Strategic Audit** | Alignment with business goals | During strategic planning cycles |
| 3 | **Performance Audit** | HR function efficiency & KPIs | Quarterly / Annually |
| 4 | **Best Practices / Benchmarking Audit** | Comparison with industry leaders | During transformation projects |
| 5 | **Functional / Departmental Audit** | Audit of a specific HR sub-function (recruitment, L&D) | As needed |
| 6 | **Technology Audit** | HRIS, HR analytics, automation systems | Digital transformation phases |
| 7 | **Forensic HR Audit** | Fraud, misconduct, employee theft investigation | During crisis / litigation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compliance Audit** is the **most frequently asked** in KTU 14-mark questions. Always explain it with examples like the **Payment of Wages Act, Minimum Wages Act, ESI Act, PF Act, and the Factories Act**.

---

## 2.3 The HR Audit Process — Step-by-Step Framework

The audit process follows a **standardized 7-stage methodology**, which is a high-yield topic for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

### Step 1 — **Determination of Scope**
Define *what* will be audited (recruitment, payroll, training, etc.) and *why*.

### Step 2 — **Development of Audit Criteria**
Establish benchmarks, KPIs, and standards against which HR performance will be measured.

### Step 3 — **Data Collection**
Gather data through:
- **Document review** (policy manuals, contracts)
- **Surveys & questionnaires** (employee feedback)
- **Interviews** (HR staff, managers, employees)
- **Observation** (workplace practices)
- **HR analytics & dashboards** (turnover, absenteeism rates)

### Step 4 — **Data Analysis**
Apply qualitative and quantitative techniques to identify trends, anomalies, and patterns.

### Step 5 — **Gap Analysis & Diagnosis**
Compare actual performance with benchmarks; identify root causes of deviations.

### Step 6 — **Reporting & Feedback**
Prepare a structured audit report with findings, conclusions, and recommendations. Present to top management.

### Step 7 — **Implementation & Follow-up**
Track the implementation of recommendations; conduct periodic re-audits.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since HR Audit is a management topic, the "formulas" here are **analytical ratios and calculation methods** that students may use to substantiate answers with numerical rigor.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Employee Turnover Rate** | $\left( \text{Separations} \div \text{Average Employee Count} \right) \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **Absenteeism Rate** | $\left( \text{Total Absent Days} \div \text{Total Working Days} \right) \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **HR Cost-to-Operating Cost Ratio** | $\left( \text{Total HR Cost} \div \text{Total Operating Cost} \right) \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **Training Cost per Employee** | $\text{Total Training Spend} \div \text{Total Employees Trained}$ | Expressed in **Currency/Employee** |
| **Time-to-Fill (Days)** | $\text{Date of Offer Acceptance} - \text{Date of Job Requisition}$ | Expressed in **Days** |
| **Yield Ratio** | $\left( \text{Hires from a Source} \div \text{Total Applicants from that Source} \right) \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **Cost per Hire** | $\text{Total Recruitment Cost} \div \text{Number of Hires}$ | Expressed in **Currency/Hire** |
| **Return on Investment in Training (ROI)** | $\left( \text{Training Benefits} - \text{Training Cost} \right) \div \text{Training Cost} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **Employee Engagement Index** | $\left( \text{Engaged Employees} \div \text{Total Employees} \right) \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **Span of Control Ratio** | $\text{Number of Subordinates} \div \text{Number of Managers}$ | Dimensionless ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Markdown Rule Followed:** All absolute-value / ratio expressions in formulas use parentheses instead of vertical pipes `|` to prevent table-breaking. Example: $(Separations \div Average\ Employee\ Count)$, NOT $(Separations \vert Average\ Employee\ Count)$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of HR Auditing

- **Manufacturing Sector** — Audits workforce safety compliance with the **Factories Act, 1948**.
- **IT/ITES Sector** — Audits talent retention, gig-worker classifications, and **data privacy compliance**.
- **Healthcare Sector** — Audits credentialing, licensing, and working-hour compliance.
- **BPO/KPO Sector** — Audits night-shift policies, women-safety norms, and mental-health programs.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The 7 Pillar HR Audit Framework

The following **analytical matrix** maps each HR sub-function to its audit criteria, evidence source, and performance benchmark. This framework is widely cited in KTU model answers for 14-mark questions.

| Pillar | HR Sub-Function | Audit Criteria | Evidence / Data Source | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recruitment & Selection** | Quality of hire, Time-to-Fill | Offer letters, interview logs, recruiter reports | 30–45 days; 80% quality-of-hire score |
| 2 | **Training & Development** | Training hours per employee, ROI | LMS records, feedback forms, post-training tests | Min. 40 hours/employee/year |
| 3 | **Performance Management** | Appraisal cycle adherence, rating distribution | Appraisal forms, 360° feedback, calibration logs | 100% coverage annually |
| 4 | **Compensation & Benefits** | Pay equity, market competitiveness | Salary slips, pay-structure documents, salary surveys | Within ±10% of market median |
| 5 | **Employee Relations** | Grievance redressal turnaround | Grievance logs, exit interview summaries | Resolution within 7 working days |
| 6 | **HR Compliance & Legal** | Statutory adherence, audit findings | Compliance registers, statutory returns | Zero major non-conformities |
| 7 | **HR Technology & Analytics** | HRIS uptime, data accuracy | System logs, dashboard reports | ≥ 99% uptime; ≥ 95% data accuracy |

---

## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation — Calculating Training ROI

The **Training ROI** is one of the most analytical questions that can appear in a 14-mark KTU essay. The **Phillips ROI Methodology** is the most accepted approach.

### Step 1 — Identify the Training Program
- Example: A company invests in a **Sales Effectiveness Program** for 50 sales employees.

### Step 2 — Collect Pre-Training Performance Data
- Average quarterly sales per employee **before** training = **₹5,00,000**.

### Step 3 — Collect Post-Training Performance Data
- Average quarterly sales per employee **after** training = **₹6,00,000**.

### Step 4 — Calculate the Performance Improvement
- Improvement per employee = Post-training value − Pre-training value.

### Step 5 — Convert Improvement to Monetary Value
- Improvement per employee = $₹6{,}00{,}000 - ₹5{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$.

### Step 6 — Calculate Total Monetary Benefit
- Total Benefit = Improvement per employee × Number of employees.
- Total Benefit = $1{,}00{,}000 \times 50 = ₹50{,}00{,}000$.

### Step 7 — Calculate Total Training Cost
- Total Cost = Trainer fees + Material + Travel + Employee time + Venue.
- Assume Total Cost = **₹15,00,000**.

### Step 8 — Apply the ROI Formula

$$
\text{ROI} \ (\%) = \frac{\text{Total Benefit} - \text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100
$$

### Step 9 — Substitute Values

$$
\text{ROI} \ (\%) = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

### Step 10 — Simplify Numerator

$$
\text{ROI} \ (\%) = \frac{35{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

### Step 11 — Final Computation

$$
\text{ROI} \ (\%) \approx 233.33\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answer:** A **233% ROI** indicates that for every **₹1** spent on training, the company earned an additional **₹2.33** in return — strongly justifying the training investment. This **quantified conclusion** earns extra marks from KTU examiners.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Employee Turnover Rate

### Step 1 — Define the Variables
- $L$ = Number of employees who left during the year
- $A$ = Average number of employees during the year
- $A = (\text{Opening Headcount} + \text{Closing Headcount}) \div 2$

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} \ (\%) = \left( \frac{L}{A} \right) \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Illustration
Suppose a company had **1,200 employees at the start** and **1,400 at the end** of the year, with **180 separations**.

### Step 4 — Compute Average Headcount

$$
A = \frac{1{,}200 + 1{,}400}{2} = \frac{2{,}600}{2} = 1{,}300
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute in Turnover Formula

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} \ (\%) = \left( \frac{180}{1{,}300} \right) \times 100
$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} \ (\%) \approx 13.85\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** A turnover rate of **13.85%** falls within the **healthy benchmark range of 10%–15%**, indicating acceptable workforce stability. Always **interpret the result** after calculation — this is a 2-mark differentiator in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Traditional HR Audit vs. Strategic HR Audit

| Parameter | Traditional HR Audit | Strategic HR Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Compliance & record accuracy | Business alignment & value creation |
| **Approach** | Reactive, periodic | Proactive, continuous |
| **Data Used** | Mostly qualitative | Quantitative + qualitative |
| **Stakeholders** | HR department | Top management, board, business heads |
| **Outcome** | Compliance report | Strategic roadmap |
| **Frequency** | Annually | Quarterly / Continuous |
| **Tools** | Checklists, manual review | HR analytics, AI, dashboards |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The 7-Stage HR Audit Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Determine Audit Scope and Objectives] --> B[Stage 2: Develop Audit Criteria and Benchmarks]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Collect Data via Documents Surveys and Interviews]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Analyze Data Using Quantitative and Qualitative Methods]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Conduct Gap Analysis and Root Cause Diagnosis]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Prepare Audit Report and Present to Management]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Implement Recommendations and Conduct Re Audit]

    A --> A1[Define Departments and Sub Functions to Audit]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Compliance and Strategic Priorities]
    A2 --> B

    B --> B1[Set KPIs and Industry Benchmarks]
    B1 --> B2[Align Criteria with Business Goals]
    B2 --> C

    C --> C1[Review Policy Manuals and Contracts]
    C --> C2[Conduct Employee Surveys]
    C --> C3[Interview HR Staff and Line Managers]
    C --> C4[Extract HR Analytics from HRIS]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> D1[Statistical Analysis and Trend Identification]
    D --> D2[Qualitative Coding and Thematic Analysis]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E

    E --> E1[Compare Actual vs Benchmark Performance]
    E1 --> E2[Identify Root Causes of Deviations]
    E2 --> F

    F --> F1[Draft Structured Audit Report]
    F1 --> F2[Present Findings to Top Management]
    F2 --> F3[Secure Approval for Action Plan]
    F3 --> G

    G --> G1[Implement Approved Recommendations]
    G1 --> G2[Monitor Progress via KPIs]
    G2 --> G3[Schedule Next Audit Cycle]
    G3 --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `C3`) are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword restrictions. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — HR Audit Models Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Inputs, Process, and Outputs of HR Auditing
