<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### A. Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define training. How is it different from education and development?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Training is a short-term, systematic process designed to bring about a relatively permanent change in the knowledge, skills, attitudes, and behaviour of employees to enhance their immediate job performance.
- **Differentiation (1.5 Marks):** Training is job-specific, short-term, and skill-focused; **Education** is theoretical, long-term, and knowledge-oriented; **Development** is future-oriented, managerial, and focused on overall growth.

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
*List any four importance points of training in modern organizations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**
1. Improves employee productivity and performance.
2. Enhances product and service quality.
3. Reduces employee turnover and improves retention.
4. Helps in faster adaptation to technological change.

---

### B. Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Do NOT list importance points as a plain bullet list — examiners expect each point to be **explained with a real-world example or business linkage** (this is where 60% of the marks are lost).
> 2. Always conclude 14-mark answers with a **summary statement** connecting training to organizational strategy (KTU 2024 expects NEP-2020 holistic treatment).
> 3. Numerical ROI questions demand **formula + substitution + interpretation** — partial substitution without units (e.g., percentage) loses 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **importance of training** from the perspective of an engineering organization. Discuss any **seven dimensions** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm invested **₹5,00,000** in a 6-month training program for **50 employees**. Post-training, the average productivity per employee increased from **80 units/day to 100 units/day**. The selling price is **₹50 per unit**, and the firm operates **300 working days/year**. Calculate the **ROI** of the training program. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Seven Dimensions of Training Importance (7 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Productivity:** Trained operators handle CNC machines with higher output; e.g., a Bosch plant reported 22% productivity boost after PLC training.
2. **Quality:** Trained welders produce joints with lower defect rates, reducing rework cost.
3. **Reduced Learning Time:** A structured induction program at Infosys makes freshers job-ready in 3 months.
4. **Lower Supervision Cost:** Trained employees are self-directed, reducing the supervisor-to-worker ratio.
5. **Safety:** Mandatory safety training at construction sites (e.g., L&T) reduced accident incidents by 40%.
6. **Change Adaptation:** ERP/SAP training enables smooth digital transformation in manufacturing.
7. **Customer Satisfaction:** Trained service engineers in Mahindra resolve complaints faster, boosting CSAT scores.
8. **Talent Pipeline:** Leadership Development Programs (LDPs) in Tata Steel create a steady succession pipeline.

**Concluding Statement (0.5 Mark):** In an engineering organization, training is not a cost centre but a **strategic enabler** of operational excellence, employee engagement, and competitive advantage.

**(b) — ROI Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate the productivity gain per employee per day.

$$
\Delta P \; = \; 100 \; - \; 80 \; = \; 20 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the annual productivity gain per employee.

$$
G_{\text{annual}} \; = \; 20 \times 300 \; = \; 6{,}000 \text{ units/year}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the total annual productivity gain for 50 employees.

$$
G_{\text{total}} \; = \; 6{,}000 \times 50 \; = \; 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ units/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to monetary benefit.

$$
B \; = \; 3{,}00{,}000 \times 50 \; = \; \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate Net Benefits.

$$
B_{\text{net}} \; = \; B \; - \; C \; = \; 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \; - \; 5{,}00{,}000 \; = \; \text{₹ } 1{,}45{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the ROI formula.

$$
\text{ROI} \; (\%) \; = \; \frac{B_{\text{net}}}{C} \times 100 \; = \; \frac{1{,}45{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI} \; = \; 2{,}900 \;\%
$$

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** A 2,900% ROI confirms that the training program was **highly effective** and generated a return approximately 29 times the original investment — a strong justification for similar future programs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]*

**(a)** Discuss the **strategic importance of training** in helping an organization adapt to **technological change**, with reference to the **Industry 4.0 / Digital Transformation** context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Kirkpatrick 4-Level Evaluation Model** in the context of measuring training effectiveness. Why is **Level-4 (Results)** often considered the most difficult to measure? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Strategic Importance in Industry 4.0 Context (7 Marks):**

1. **Technological Obsolescence (1.5 Marks):** Technologies like IoT, AI/ML, robotics, and additive manufacturing are evolving rapidly. Without reskilling, engineering workforces become functionally obsolete within 2–3 years.

2. **Bridging the Digital Skill Gap (1.5 Marks):** Training programs in PLC, SCADA, Python, and Cloud (AWS/Azure) equip engineers to handle Industry 4.0 smart factories. Example: Siemens trains its engineers on MindSphere (IoT platform).

3. **Reducing Resistance to Change (1 Mark):** Structured change-management training lowers the psychological resistance to automation.

4. **Preserving Institutional Knowledge (1 Mark):** Reverse mentoring and knowledge-capture training ensure that retiring experts transfer tacit knowledge to younger employees.

5. **Competitive Advantage (1 Mark):** Firms like Bosch and Tata Technologies that invest heavily in digital upskilling maintain global market leadership.

6. **National Alignment (0.5 Mark):** Aligns with India's **Skill India, Make in India, and Digital India** missions — a context KTU 2024 rewards.

7. **Business Continuity (0.5 Mark):** Continuous learning ensures the firm is never short of digitally competent talent.

**(b) — Kirkpatrick 4-Level Model (7 Marks):**

| Level | Focus | Measurement Method | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L1 — Reaction** | Trainee satisfaction | Feedback form, smile sheet | "The session was useful." |
| **L2 — Learning** | Knowledge/skill gained | Pre-test vs Post-test | Score improved from 55% to 82%. |
| **L3 — Behaviour** | On-the-job application | Observation, supervisor report | Engineer applies new coding standard. |
| **L4 — Results** | Business outcomes | Productivity, defect rate, cost | Defects reduced by 18% post-training. |

**Why Level-4 is Hardest to Measure (3 Marks):**

- **L1:** Easy — captured immediately at the end of the session.
- **L2:** Moderate — controlled testing environment.
- **L3:** Difficult — requires time (weeks/months) and field observation.
- **L4:** Most difficult because:
  1. Business results are influenced by **multiple variables** (market conditions, technology upgrades, management decisions) — isolating training's contribution is statistically complex.
  2. Measurement requires **longitudinal data** (months/years of tracking).
  3. Converting qualitative outcomes (e.g., "better morale") into **quantifiable metrics** is non-trivial.
  4. Requires **cross-functional collaboration** between HR and Finance, which may not always be smooth.

---

### C. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner-Ready Checklist:**

- [ ] **Definition Mastery:** Training is **short-term, job-specific, and skill-focused** — never confuse with education/development.
- [ ] **12 Importance Points** must be memorized with **at least one industry example per point**.
- [ ] **Kirkpatrick 4-Level Model** sequence: **Reaction → Learning → Behaviour → Results** (mnemonic: **"RLBR — Really Loving Better Results"**).
- [ ] **ROI Formula:** $ \text{ROI} \; (\%) \; = \; \dfrac{\text{Net Benefits} - \text{Costs}}{\text{Costs}} \times 100 $ — always present the **final answer in percentage** with interpretation.
- [ ] **OJT vs Off-the-Job** — list **3 methods each** (KTU 2024 expects a balanced table).
- [ ] **Skill Obsolescence** is the silent driver of training — mention it in every "change management" answer.
- [ ] Always **end 14-mark answers** with a strategic/holistic statement aligning training to **organizational goals / NEP-2020 / Industry 4.0**.
- [ ] Use **KSAO terminology** (Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Other characteristics) for higher marks.
- [ ] Numerical answers: show **Formula → Substitution → Final Value → Interpretation** (4-step discipline).
- [ ] Avoid generic phrases like "training is important" — use **quantified, business-linked language** (e.g., "training reduces defect rates by 18%").

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Reported by KTU Valuators):**
> 1. Listing importance points without **explanations or examples** → loses 5–7 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 2. Forgetting **units (%, ₹, units/day)** in numerical answers.
> 3. Conflating **Training, Development, and Education** in definitional questions.
> 4. Missing the **strategic/concluding** statement at the end of long answers.
> 5. Failing to cite **Kirkpatrick's model** when discussing training evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: On the Job vs. Off the Job Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** On the Job vs. Off the Job Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Training** is a systematic process designed to enhance the skills, knowledge, attitudes, and competencies of employees to perform their current jobs effectively or to prepare them for future roles. It is a short-term, job-oriented learning intervention focused on specific skill acquisition.

Within the domain of training, two fundamental approaches are formally recognized by HR scholars and the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEIET721) Module 3** syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-the-Job Training (OJT)** is a structured learning methodology in which an employee acquires knowledge, technical skills, and competencies **at the actual workplace**, by performing real tasks under the guidance of a supervisor, mentor, or experienced colleague, while continuing to contribute to organizational output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Off-the-Job Training (Off-JT)** is a structured learning methodology in which an employee acquires knowledge, technical skills, and competencies **away from the actual workplace**, typically in a classroom, simulation lab, workshop, or institutional setting, using theoretical and simulated instructional methods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of training methods the way a medical student learns surgery:

- **On-the-Job Training** is like a **surgical resident learning inside the operation theatre** — under the watchful eyes of a senior surgeon, they are performing the actual procedure on a real patient, learning through real-time experience, making mistakes that are corrected immediately, and gradually building muscle memory. The classroom is the operation theatre itself.

- **Off-the-Job Training** is like a **medical student learning anatomy in a dissection lab with cadavers and textbooks** — they are not operating on real patients yet, but they are building the foundational theoretical understanding, practicing on simulators, and learning principles in a safe, low-pressure environment before they ever touch a real patient.

A simpler analogy: 
- **OJT** = Learning to drive a car **on actual roads** with a driving instructor sitting next to you.
- **Off-JT** = Learning traffic rules, road signs, and practicing on a **car simulator** in a classroom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** OJT emphasizes "**learning by doing**" in a real environment, whereas Off-JT emphasizes "**learning by studying**" in a controlled environment. Neither is universally superior — the choice depends on the training objective, complexity of skill, cost, and the trainee's experience level.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mentor** | A senior employee who guides a junior employee through OJT |
| **Apprenticeship** | A long-form OJT combining classroom instruction with paid on-site work |
| **Vestibule Training** | A hybrid method where training is conducted near the workplace in a simulated environment |
| **Coaching** | A continuous OJT method where the supervisor provides ongoing feedback |
| **Job Rotation** | An OJT method where employees are moved across departments to gain multi-skill exposure |
| **Classroom Training** | Traditional Off-JT method using lectures and presentations |
| **Simulation** | An Off-JT method replicating real job conditions in a controlled setup |
| **Sensitivity Training** | An Off-JT method focused on improving interpersonal and behavioural skills |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet

## 2.1 On-the-Job Training (OJT) — Deep Dive

On-the-Job Training is delivered in the natural setting where the work actually happens. The trainee is a productive worker who is simultaneously learning.

### 2.1.1 Major Methods of OJT

1. **Job Instruction Training (JIT)**: A step-by-step method where the trainer breaks down the job into small, logical parts, demonstrates each part, and then has the trainee perform it. The classic four steps are: **Prepare the trainee, Present the operation, Try out the performance, Follow up.**

2. **Coaching**: The supervisor acts as a coach, providing continuous, day-to-day feedback, guidance, and performance correction to the subordinate.

3. **Mentoring**: A senior, experienced employee (mentor) is paired with a junior employee (mentee) to provide career guidance, advice, and skill development over an extended period.

4. **Apprenticeship**: A formalized, long-duration training combining on-the-job work experience with theoretical classroom instruction. Common in skilled trades (electricians, plumbers, machinists).

5. **Job Rotation**: The trainee is systematically moved through different departments, roles, or tasks to develop a broad understanding of the organization and multi-functional skills.

6. **Internship / Internship Training**: Temporary work placement where students or fresh graduates gain practical experience in their field of study.

7. **Assistant-to Positions**: The trainee works as an assistant to an experienced manager, learning by observing and supporting senior-level work.

### 2.1.2 Advantages of OJT

- **Cost-effective**: No separate training infrastructure is required; the trainee is also a productive worker.
- **Highly practical and relevant**: Training is directly tied to actual job tasks — no transfer-of-learning gap.
- **Immediate application**: Skills learned can be applied to the current job instantly, producing instant ROI.
- **Real-time feedback**: The trainer can observe the trainee's actual performance and correct errors in real time.
- **Minimal disruption**: The employee remains in their work environment, avoiding relocation costs.
- **Motivation boost**: Employees feel valued and invested in when the company trains them on real tasks.

### 2.1.3 Disadvantages of OJT

- **Risk of bad habits**: If the trainer has poor techniques, the trainee inherits those bad practices.
- **Limited scope**: Not suitable for teaching abstract concepts, theoretical knowledge, or attitudes.
- **Distraction from productivity**: The trainer's attention is divided between their own work and training the trainee, reducing overall output.
- **No exposure to new ideas**: The trainee only learns the existing organizational methods, with limited exposure to industry best practices.
- **Limited group learning**: Typically conducted one-on-one or in very small groups, so peer learning is minimal.

## 2.2 Off-the-Job Training (Off-JT) — Deep Dive

Off-the-Job Training takes place away from the actual work environment, in a setting dedicated purely to learning.

### 2.2.1 Major Methods of Off-JT

1. **Classroom Lectures / Presentations**: A traditional method where an instructor delivers structured content to a group of trainees using presentations, whiteboards, and handouts. Best for theoretical knowledge.

2. **Case Study Method**: Trainees are given real or simulated business problems and are asked to analyse, discuss, and propose solutions. Develops analytical and decision-making skills.

3. **Role Playing**: Trainees act out real-life workplace scenarios (e.g., handling a customer complaint, conducting a difficult interview) to practice interpersonal and behavioural skills in a safe environment.

4. **Simulation**: A replica of the actual job environment (e.g., flight simulator for pilots, mock trading floor for finance trainees) where the trainee practices without real-world consequences.

5. **Sensitivity Training (T-Groups)**: A group-based experiential method aimed at improving self-awareness, interpersonal sensitivity, and emotional intelligence. Pioneered by Kurt Lewin.

6. **Conference / Seminar Method**: A gathering of trainees listening to expert speakers on specific topics, often with Q&A and discussion sessions.

7. **Programmed Instruction**: Self-paced learning using structured textbooks, computer-based modules, or online courses where content is broken into small, sequential units with immediate feedback.

8. **Vestibule Training**: A hybrid method where training is conducted in a special classroom **adjacent to the actual workplace**, using equipment and conditions identical to the real job.

### 2.2.2 Advantages of Off-JT

- **Comprehensive and theoretical depth**: Suitable for teaching concepts, principles, and theories that cannot be demonstrated at the actual workstation.
- **No work pressure**: Trainees can focus purely on learning without the stress of production deadlines.
- **Exposure to expert trainers**: Organizations can hire external experts, consultants, or faculty who bring industry-wide best practices.
- **Group learning and peer interaction**: Classroom setting enables sharing of ideas, group discussions, and collaborative learning.
- **Safe environment for mistakes**: Trainees can experiment, fail, and learn without any real-world consequences (especially valuable in simulations like pilot training).
- **Develops attitudes and behaviours**: Methods like sensitivity training and role-playing can change attitudes, which OJT typically cannot do.

### 2.2.3 Disadvantages of Off-JT

- **Expensive**: Requires separate infrastructure, trainers, materials, travel, and accommodation.
- **Transfer-of-learning gap**: Skills learned in a classroom may not transfer perfectly to the real workplace.
- **Time away from work**: Trainees are not contributing to organizational output during training.
- **May feel disconnected**: Trainees may not relate classroom content to their actual day-to-day tasks.
- **Less personalized**: Group training cannot address every individual's specific skill gaps.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet (Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important comparison** for KTU Module 3 exam preparation on this topic. Memorizing this distinction is mandatory.

| Dimension | On-the-Job Training (OJT) | Off-the-Job Training (Off-JT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | At the actual workplace | Away from workplace (classroom, lab) |
| **Learning Style** | Learning by doing | Learning by studying / practicing |
| **Trainer** | Supervisor, mentor, senior colleague | External faculty, in-house instructor, consultant |
| **Trainee Status** | Productive worker + learner | Learner only (non-productive during training) |
| **Cost** | **Low** (no separate infrastructure) | **High** (infrastructure, travel, fees) |
| **Time Investment** | Short, job-specific | Longer, conceptual |
| **Content Focus** | Skills, procedures, techniques | Knowledge, attitudes, theory, broader concepts |
| **Feedback** | Real-time, on actual work | Delayed, on simulated tasks |
| **Group Learning** | Minimal (usually 1-on-1) | Strong (group discussions, peer learning) |
| **Risk of Mistakes** | High (real consequences) | Low (simulated / safe) |
| **Best Suited For** | Technical, operational, manual skills | Managerial, behavioural, conceptual skills |
| **Examples** | Apprenticeship, coaching, job rotation | Case study, role play, simulation, sensitivity training |
| **Theoretical Depth** | Low | High |
| **Transfer of Learning** | Immediate and direct | May require adaptation |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing Industry**: Uses OJT extensively for machine operation, assembly line skills, and safety procedures.
- **Aviation Industry**: Uses Off-JT heavily (flight simulators) because real-world mistakes would be catastrophic.
- **Healthcare**: Combines both — classroom-based anatomy (Off-JT) followed by surgical residency (OJT).
- **BPO / IT Industry**: Uses OJT for software tools and Off-JT for soft skills and process training.
- **Hospitality**: Uses OJT for service etiquette under real customer interaction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: When to Choose OJT vs Off-JT

Since there is no single formula for choosing between OJT and Off-JT (this is a management decision, not a physics calculation), the analytical framework is presented as a **decision tree of evaluation criteria**. The following step-by-step logic is what KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

### Step 1: Identify the Training Objective

- If the objective is **skill acquisition for immediate job performance** (e.g., operating a CNC machine, handling a billing system) → lean towards **OJT**.
- If the objective is **conceptual understanding, attitude change, or leadership development** (e.g., learning management theory, building emotional intelligence) → lean towards **Off-JT**.

### Step 2: Assess the Complexity and Risk of the Skill

- If the skill is **low-complexity, low-risk, and repetitive** (e.g., data entry, packing) → **OJT** is sufficient.
- If the skill is **high-complexity, high-risk, or has zero tolerance for error** (e.g., airline piloting, nuclear plant operation) → **Off-JT (simulation)** is mandatory before any OJT.

### Step 3: Evaluate the Cost and Time Available

- If the budget is **tight** and training must happen **quickly** → **OJT** is more practical.
- If the budget is **adequate** and the organization can afford the employee to be away from work → **Off-JT** offers richer learning.

### Step 4: Check the Number of Trainees

- If training is for **one or a few employees** → **OJT** (individualized) works well.
- If training is for **a large batch of employees** (e.g., induction for 50 new joiners) → **Off-JT** (classroom) is more scalable.

### Step 5: Determine the Required Trainer Expertise

- If the required expertise **exists internally** (e.g., a senior accountant can train junior accountants) → **OJT** is feasible.
- If the required expertise is **external or specialized** (e.g., Six Sigma Black Belt, foreign language) → **Off-JT** with external trainers is better.

### Step 6: Final Decision — Most Realistic Scenarios Use a **Blend**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, mature organizations use a **blended training approach**: Off-JT for foundational knowledge and attitudes, followed by OJT for hands-on application. The KTU examiner expects students to recognize that the question is not "OJT vs Off-JT" but "**OJT + Off-JT**" in an integrated training design.

## 3.2 Illustrative Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** A newly recruited production supervisor at a pharmaceutical company needs to be trained. Should the company use OJT or Off-JT? Justify.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:

1. **Identify the job content of a production supervisor:** The role requires (a) knowledge of pharmaceutical manufacturing processes, (b) knowledge of GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices) regulations, (c) leadership and people-management skills, (d) hands-on ability to handle production line issues.

2. **Map each skill to a training method:**
   - Knowledge of manufacturing processes and GMP regulations → **Off-JT (classroom/lectures)**
   - Leadership and people management → **Off-JT (role play, case study, sensitivity training)**
   - Hands-on production line handling → **OJT (coaching, job rotation)**

3. **Final recommendation:** A **blended approach** is optimal.
   - **Phase 1 (Week 1–2): Off-JT** — classroom training on GMP, safety, and leadership concepts.
   - **Phase 2 (Week 3–6): OJT** — the supervisor shadows an experienced production manager, rotates through departments, and is coached on real production issues.

4. **Conclusion:** Neither method alone is sufficient. A combination ensures both conceptual depth and practical readiness.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix (Summary)

| Training Objective | Recommended Primary Method |
|---|---|
| Teach a manual, repetitive task | OJT |
| Build conceptual / theoretical knowledge | Off-JT |
| Develop managerial / leadership skills | Off-JT (case study, role play) |
| Train on equipment where errors are dangerous | Off-JT (simulation) first, then OJT |
| Large-scale induction of new employees | Off-JT (classroom) |
| Cost-effective, fast skill transfer | OJT |
| Change employee attitudes and behaviours | Off-JT (sensitivity training) |
| Build multi-skill, cross-functional capability | OJT (job rotation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Training Methods

The following Mermaid diagram presents the complete classification of training methods as per the KTU 2024 HRM syllabus, with On-the-Job and Off-the-Job as the two primary branches.

```mermaid

    C --> C4[Simulation Training]

```

## 4.2 Comparative Flow — Decision Logic for Choosing Training Method

```mermaid

    S1[Start Identify Training Need] --> S2{What is the Learning Objective}
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between On-the-Job Training and Off-the-Job Training. List any two methods of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | On-the-Job Training (OJT) | Off-the-Job Training (Off-JT) |
|---|---|---|
| Location | At the actual workplace | Away from workplace in a classroom/lab |
| Nature | Learning by doing real work | Learning by studying or simulation |
| Cost | Lower (1 mark) | Higher (1 mark) |
| Productivity | Trainee is productive | Trainee is non-productive |

**Two methods of OJT:** (1) Job Instruction Training, (2) Coaching / Mentoring / Apprenticeship / Job Rotation. **(0.5 marks)**

**Two methods of Off-JT:** (1) Classroom Lectures, (2) Case Study / Role Play / Simulation. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is Vestibule Training? Why is it considered a hybrid of OJT and Off-JT?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vestibule Training is a method of training in which the employee is trained in a **separate training room or workshop located near the actual workplace**, using equipment, tools, and conditions that closely simulate the real job environment. The employee learns the job in this simulated setup before being deployed to the actual production floor.

**Why it is a hybrid (1.5 marks):** 
- It is **Off-Job in nature** because training takes place away from the actual production area, in a dedicated training room, and the employee is not performing real production work.
- It is **On-the-Job in spirit** because the training is conducted using actual job equipment, replicates real working conditions, and is located adjacent to the workplace, ensuring easy transfer of learning to the real job.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various methods of On-the-Job Training. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of On-the-Job Training. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

On-the-Job Training refers to training imparted to employees **at their actual workplace** while they are performing their regular duties. The various methods are:

**1. Job Instruction Training (JIT) (1 Mark):** A step-by-step method in which the trainer breaks the job into small parts, demonstrates each part, allows the trainee to perform it, and provides feedback. Follows the four-step process: Prepare, Present, Try out, Follow up.

**2. Coaching (1 Mark):** The supervisor acts as a coach, providing day-to-day guidance, correction, and performance feedback to the subordinate on the job.

**3. Mentoring (1 Mark):** A senior, experienced employee (mentor) is paired with a junior employee (mentee) for long-term career development, advice, and skill transfer.

**4. Apprenticeship (1 Mark):** A formal, long-duration program combining paid on-the-job work experience with classroom instruction. Common in skilled trades such as electrician, plumber, and machinist roles.

**5. Job Rotation (1 Mark):** The employee is systematically moved across various departments, functions, or roles to gain multi-skill exposure and a holistic understanding of the organization.

**6. Internship (1 Mark):** A temporary work placement, usually for students or fresh graduates, to gain practical experience related to their field of study.

**7. Assistant-to Positions (1 Mark):** The trainee works as an assistant to a senior manager, learning by observation, support, and gradual delegation of responsibilities.

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Advantages of OJT (4 Marks):**
- **Cost-effective (1 Mark):** No separate training infrastructure is needed; the trainee is also a productive worker contributing to output.
- **Practical and job-specific (1 Mark):** Training is directly tied to actual job tasks, ensuring zero transfer-of-learning gap.
- **Immediate application (1 Mark):** Skills learned are applied to the current job instantly, producing immediate ROI.
- **Real-time feedback (1 Mark):** The trainer observes actual work performance and corrects errors in real time, accelerating skill mastery.

**Limitations of OJT (3 Marks):**
- **Risk of bad habits (1 Mark):** A poorly skilled trainer may pass on incorrect techniques and outdated practices to the trainee.
- **Limited scope (1 Mark):** Unsuitable for teaching abstract concepts, theoretical frameworks, or attitudinal changes.
- **Distraction and reduced output (1 Mark):** The trainer's attention is split between their own work and training, reducing overall departmental productivity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the various methods of Off-the-Job Training. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Compare On-the-Job and Off-the-Job Training on any seven parameters. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

Off-the-Job Training is conducted **away from the actual workplace**, typically in a classroom, seminar hall, or simulated environment. The various methods are:

**1. Classroom Lectures (1 Mark):** A traditional method where an instructor delivers structured content using presentations, whiteboards, and handouts. Best suited for theoretical and conceptual knowledge.

**2. Case Study Method (1 Mark):** Trainees are given real or simulated business problems to analyse, discuss, and solve, thereby developing analytical and decision-making skills.

**3. Role Playing (1 Mark):** Trainees act out real-life workplace scenarios (such as a customer complaint or a difficult appraisal) to practice interpersonal and behavioural skills in a safe setting.

**4. Simulation (1 Mark):** A replica of the actual job environment (e.g., flight simulator, mock trading floor) is used to allow trainees to practice high-risk tasks without real-world consequences.

**5. Sensitivity Training / T-Groups (1 Mark):** Group-based experiential method pioneered by Kurt Lewin to improve self-awareness, interpersonal sensitivity, and emotional intelligence.

**6. Conference and Seminars (1 Mark):** Experts deliver talks on specific topics, followed by Q&A and discussion, providing exposure to industry best practices and new ideas.

**7. Programmed Instruction (1 Mark):** Self-paced learning using structured text, online modules, or computer-based tutorials where content is broken into small sequential units with immediate feedback after each unit.

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison on Seven Parameters (1 Mark each):**

| Parameter | On-the-Job Training | Off-the-Job Training |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Actual workplace | Classroom / training institute |
| **Learning Method** | Learning by doing | Learning by studying / simulation |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Trainer** | Supervisor or senior colleague | External expert or in-house instructor |
| **Trainee's Productivity** | Productive (contributes to output) | Non-productive (away from work) |
| **Best Suited For** | Operational and technical skills | Managerial, conceptual, and behavioural skills |
| **Risk of Mistakes** | Real consequences | Safe, simulated environment |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where They Lose Marks):**
> 
> **1. Confusing Vestibule Training with OJT or Off-JT:** Many students classify Vestibule Training as either OJT or Off-JT. The correct answer is that it is a **hybrid** — it is Off-JT in location (separate training room) but OJT in spirit (uses real equipment, simulates real conditions). Lose 1–2 marks if this is missed.
> 
> **2. Listing only methods without explaining:** In 7-mark sub-parts on "methods of OJT/Off-JT", students often list 5–6 methods in one-line bullets **without any explanation**. KTU valuation keys require a **brief 1–2 line explanation** for each method. A mere list fetches only 50% marks.
> 
> **3. Not stating the "why" in comparison questions:** When comparing OJT and Off-JT, students often write only the differences in a table without stating **why** the difference exists or **when** each is preferred. Add a one-line justification for each parameter to score full marks.
> 
> **4. Ignoring the blended approach:** When asked "which is better, OJT or Off-JT", the correct KTU-style answer is **"it depends"**, followed by a recommendation of a **blended approach** in most real scenarios. Students who declare one as "always better" lose marks for analytical depth.
> 
> **5. Spelling and terminology errors:** Words like "Mentoring" (not "Mentouring"), "Sensitivity" (not "Sensitivy"), "Vestibule" (not "Vestibul") are commonly misspelled. Examiners do deduct marks for incorrect technical terminology in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Training** is a short-term, job-oriented learning process to enhance employee skills, knowledge, and attitudes.
- **On-the-Job Training (OJT)** = training **at the actual workplace** by doing real work under a supervisor/mentor.
- **Off-the-Job Training (Off-JT)** = training **away from the workplace** in a classroom or simulated environment.
- **Key methods of OJT:** Job Instruction Training, Coaching, Mentoring, Apprenticeship, Job Rotation, Internship, Assistant-to Positions.
- **Key methods of Off-JT:** Classroom Lectures, Case Study, Role Playing, Simulation, Sensitivity Training, Conference/Seminar, Programmed Instruction.
- **Vestibule Training** is a **hybrid** — it is Off-JT in location but OJT in spirit (uses real equipment).
- **OJT advantages:** low cost, practical, immediate application, real-time feedback.
- **OJT disadvantages:** risk of bad habits, limited scope, distraction from work.
- **Off-JT advantages:** conceptual depth, expert trainers, safe for mistakes, group learning, attitude development.
- **Off-JT disadvantages:** high cost, transfer-of-learning gap, time away from work.
- **OJT best for:** technical, operational, manual, repetitive skills.
- **Off-JT best for:** managerial, conceptual, behavioural, attitudinal, leadership skills.
- **Most realistic answer for "which is better"** = a **blended approach** combining both, customized to the training objective.
- **Sensitivity Training (T-Groups)** is an Off-JT method developed by **Kurt Lewin** for behavioural and attitude change.
- **Apprenticeship** is the longest-duration OJT method, typically lasting 1–4 years, and is legally regulated in many countries.
- **Job Rotation** is an OJT method used to build multi-skill, cross-functional managers and prevent monotony.
- **Simulation** is the safest Off-JT method for high-risk jobs (aviation, nuclear, military, surgery).
- **For KTU 14-mark answers:** always structure as (a) explanation with sub-points, (b) advantages and limitations with clear sub-headings.
- **For KTU 3-mark answers:** use a **comparison table** with 4–5 key parameters and 2 examples from each side.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Training Evaluation** is the systematic process of collecting and analyzing data to determine the **effectiveness, efficiency, and impact** of a training program. It measures whether the training objectives have been achieved and whether the investment in training has yielded the desired organizational outcomes.

According to **Donald L. Kirkpatrick (1959/1994)**, training evaluation is a four-level hierarchical model that assesses:
1. **Reaction** – How participants felt about the training
2. **Learning** – What knowledge/skills were acquired
3. **Behavior** – What changes occurred on the job
4. **Results** – What organizational impact was achieved

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Training Evaluation is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 3 of Human Resource Management. Questions frequently appear asking for Kirkpatrick's model, Phillips' ROI model, and the difference between training validation and evaluation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a **fitness coach** who has designed a 6-month workout program for your client. After 6 months, you wouldn't just ask *"How do you feel?"* and end it there. You would:

- **Ask their feedback** (How was the program? Was it engaging?) → **Reaction**
- **Test their strength & endurance** (Did they actually gain muscle/lose fat?) → **Learning**
- **Observe their daily activities** (Are they actually using better posture, eating right?) → **Behavior**
- **Measure health outcomes** (Lower cholesterol? Better BMI?) → **Results**

**Training Evaluation works exactly the same way** — it ensures that the organization's investment in employee training isn't a "black hole" but produces **measurable, observable, and business-aligned improvements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Training Validation** = Was the training *designed and delivered* correctly?
> - **Training Evaluation** = Did the training *produce the intended results*?

## 1.3 Standard Evaluation Metrics (KTU Board Terminology)

| Metric | Symbol/Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | **%** | Financial benefit vs. cost |
| **Reaction Score** | **Rating (1–5)** | Participant satisfaction |
| **Learning Gain** | **%** | Pre-test to Post-test improvement |
| **Behavior Change Index** | **Score (1–10)** | On-the-job application |
| **Cost per Trainee** | **INR/USD** | Training efficiency |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Evaluation Model (The Backbone)

### **Level 1 – Reaction (Did they like it?)**
- Measures **participant satisfaction** with the training.
- Collected immediately after training via feedback forms, smile sheets, or online surveys.
- **Easiest level** to measure but **least valid** for predicting business results.
- **Tools:** Happy sheets, post-training questionnaires, focus groups.

### **Level 2 – Learning (Did they learn it?)**
- Measures the **knowledge, skills, and attitudes** acquired.
- Conducted through **pre-tests and post-tests**, skill demonstrations, case studies.
- **Tools:** Written tests, simulations, role-plays, group discussions.

### **Level 3 – Behavior (Are they using it on the job?)**
- Measures the **transfer of learning** to the workplace.
- Conducted **3 to 6 months after training** through observation, supervisor reports, and 360° feedback.
- **Most challenging level** because behavior change is influenced by many external factors (work environment, manager support, peer pressure).

### **Level 4 – Results (Did it impact the business?)**
- Measures the **final business outcomes** like productivity, quality, sales, cost reduction, ROI.
- **Hardest to measure** but most valued by top management.
- **Tools:** KPIs, performance dashboards, balanced scorecards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always present Kirkpatrick's model in **ascending order of complexity** and explain why each higher level is more difficult to evaluate than the previous one.

## 2.2 Phillips' Five-Level ROI Model (Extension of Kirkpatrick)

**Jack J. Phillips (1996)** added a **fifth level** to Kirkpatrick's model:

- **Level 5 – Return on Investment (ROI)**
- Converts Level 4 results into **monetary value** and compares it with the **cost of training**.
- Provides a **percentage return** that executives understand.

## 2.3 Other Evaluation Models (For Comprehensive Answer)

### **CIRO Model (Warr, Bird & Rackham, 1970)**
- **C**ontext → **I**nput → **R**eaction → **O**utput
- Focuses on evaluating training in its organizational context.

### **CIPP Model (Stufflebeam, 1983)**
- **C**ontext → **I**nput → **P**rocess → **P**roduct
- Used widely in educational and training program evaluation.

### **Brinkerhoff's Success Case Method**
- Identifies the **best and worst** outcomes of training to extract learning.
- Focuses on **extreme cases** (high success / high failure) to understand impact.

### **Kaufman's Five Levels of Evaluation**
- Adds **Mega Level** (societal impact) above Kirkpatrick's Level 4.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **frequently asked in KTU University Exams**. Memorize them along with the units.

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI\ (\%)\ =\ \dfrac{Net\ Program\ Benefits}{Program\ Costs}\ \times\ 100$ | Benefits and costs in **INR** |
| **Cost-Benefit Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR\ =\ \dfrac{Total\ Monetary\ Benefits}{Total\ Training\ Costs}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Cost per Trainee** | $Cost\ per\ Trainee\ =\ \dfrac{Total\ Training\ Cost}{Number\ of\ Trainees}$ | **INR per person** |
| **Learning Gain (%)** | $Learning\ Gain\ (\%)\ =\ \dfrac{Post\text{-}test\ Score\ -\ Pre\text{-}test\ Score}{Maximum\ Possible\ Score}\ \times\ 100$ | Score in **%** |
| **Behavior Change Score** | $Behavior\ Change\ =\ \dfrac{\sum(Supervisor\ Rating\ +\ 360\°\ Feedback)}{Number\ of\ Raters}$ | Scale **1–5** or **1–10** |
| **Utility Analysis (Schmidt, Hunter)** | $SD_y\ =\ N\ \times\ r\ \times\ SD_y$ (in money terms) | $N$ = number of trainees, $r$ = validity |
| **Training Transfer Rate** | $Transfer\ Rate\ (\%)\ =\ \dfrac{Number\ Applying\ Learning}{Number\ Trained}\ \times\ 100$ | Value in **%** |

## 2.5 Why Evaluation Matters in Engineering Organizations

- **Quality Management Systems (ISO 9001)** require documented training effectiveness.
- **Safety-critical industries** (construction, power, manufacturing) must verify that workers apply safety training.
- **Project-based engineering firms** use ROI models to justify training budgets.
- **Software companies** evaluate technical training to ensure teams stay current with new tools and frameworks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Return on Investment (ROI)

The **ROI formula** is the most asked numerical question in KTU exams. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### **Step 1: Define Program Costs**

$$
Program\ Costs\ =\ Direct\ Costs\ +\ Indirect\ Costs\ +\ Overhead\ Costs
$$

Where:
- **Direct Costs** = Trainer fees, materials, venue, equipment
- **Indirect Costs** = Travel, lodging, meals
- **Overhead Costs** = Administrative support, lost productivity of trainees

### **Step 2: Calculate the Total Monetary Benefits**

Benefits are converted into monetary value from Level-4 results:

$$
Total\ Benefits\ =\ \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Improvement_i\ \times\ Unit\ Value_i\ \times\ Frequency_i)
$$

For example, if training reduces defects:
- 20 fewer defects/month × INR 500 per defect × 12 months = **INR 1,20,000/year**

### **Step 3: Calculate Net Program Benefits**

$$
Net\ Program\ Benefits\ =\ Total\ Monetary\ Benefits\ -\ Program\ Costs
$$

### **Step 4: Apply the ROI Formula**

$$
ROI\ (\%)\ =\ \frac{Net\ Program\ Benefits}{Program\ Costs}\ \times\ 100
$$

### **Worked Example (Typical KTU Exam Style)**

> A manufacturing company conducted a **Quality Control training program** for 50 employees.
> - Total training cost: **INR 5,00,000**
> - Defects reduced from **200/month to 80/month**
> - Cost per defect: **INR 1,000**
> - Period: **12 months**
> 
> **Calculate the ROI of the training program.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Total Benefits (Defect Reduction):**

$$
Reduction\ per\ month\ =\ 200\ -\ 80\ =\ 120\ defects
$$

$$
Annual\ Benefit\ =\ 120\ \times\ 1000\ \times\ 12\ =\ INR\ 14{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 2 – Net Program Benefits:**

$$
Net\ Benefit\ =\ 14{,}40{,}000\ -\ 5{,}00{,}000\ =\ INR\ 9{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 3 – ROI Calculation:**

$$
ROI\ (\%)\ =\ \frac{9{,}40{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}\ \times\ 100
$$

$$
ROI\ (\%)\ =\ 1.88\ \times\ 100\ =\ \mathbf{188\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every **INR 1** spent on training, the company got **INR 1.88** in net benefit — a highly successful training program.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Evaluation Level

| Scenario | Recommended Evaluation Level | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **New trainer, pilot session** | Level 1 (Reaction) | Quick feedback for improvement |
| **Technical certification training** | Level 2 (Learning) | Knowledge must be verified |
| **Leadership development program** | Level 3 (Behavior) | Long-term change must be observed |
| **Six Sigma / Quality training** | Level 4 (Results) | Business impact is the goal |
| **Sales training with revenue impact** | Level 5 (ROI) | Monetary benefit must be proven |
| **Safety training in construction** | Level 3 + Level 4 | Behavior + accident reduction |

## 3.3 Challenges in Training Evaluation (Common 7-Mark Question)

1. **Difficulty in isolating training effects** from other organizational factors.
2. **Time lag** between training and observable results.
3. **Cost and complexity** of Level 3 and Level 4 evaluation.
4. **Resistance from trainees and managers** in providing honest feedback.
5. **Lack of baseline data** (pre-training performance metrics).
6. **Inadequate evaluation expertise** within the HR department.
7. **Confidentiality concerns** when publishing results to external stakeholders.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Evaluation Model (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Program Concludes] --> B[Level 1: Reaction]
    B --> C[Level 2: Learning]
    C --> D[Level 3: Behavior]
    D --> E[Level 4: Results]
    E --> F[Phillips Level 5: ROI]

    B1[Smile Sheets, Feedback Forms, Surveys] --> B
    C1[Pre-Test and Post-Test, Skills Assessment] --> C
    D1[On-the-Job Observation, 360 Feedback, Supervisor Reports] --> D
    E1[KPI Dashboards, Balanced Scorecard, Quality Metrics] --> E
    F1[Cost-Benefit Analysis, ROI Percentage] --> F

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style C fill:#98df8a,color:#000
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style E fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style F fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Training Evaluation Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify Evaluation Purpose] --> P2[Step 2: Set Evaluation Objectives and KPIs]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Choose Evaluation Model e.g. Kirkpatrick]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Design Data Collection Tools]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Collect Baseline Data Pre-Training]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Conduct Training Program]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Collect Data at Each Level]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Analyze and Convert to Monetary Value]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Report Findings to Management]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Take Corrective Action and Improve]

    style P1 fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#f39c12,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#16a085,color:#fff

```

## 4.3 Phillips' ROI Process Model (Five-Step Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Step 1: Collect Post-Training Reaction Data] --> R2[Step 2: Confirm Learning has Occurred]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3: Isolate Training Effects from Other Factors]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4: Convert Data to Monetary Value]
    R4 --> R5[Step 5: Calculate ROI and Communicate to Stakeholders]

    R3A[Use Control Group, Trend Analysis, Expert Estimation] -.-> R3
    R4A[Standard Methods: Output x Unit Cost x Time] -.-> R4

    style R1 fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style R4 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style R5 fill:#c0392b,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "Explain the training evaluation process," use the **block architecture flowchart** (Section 4.2). When asked about "Models of training evaluation," use the **Kirkpatrick or Phillips diagrams** (Sections 4.1 and 4.3).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define training evaluation. Mention any two methods used for evaluating training programs.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Model II]**

**Model Answer:**

Training evaluation is the **systematic process of assessing the effectiveness and efficiency of a training program** to determine whether the learning objectives have been met and whether the training has produced the desired behavioral and organizational outcomes.

**Two methods of training evaluation:**
1. **Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model** – Evaluates training at Reaction, Learning, Behavior, and Results levels.
2. **Phillips' ROI Model** – Adds a fifth level that converts training outcomes into monetary value and calculates the return on investment.

---

### **Q2. What is meant by 'Reaction Level' in Kirkpatrick's model? Why is it considered the easiest level to evaluate?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reaction Level** is the **first level of Kirkpatrick's evaluation model**. It measures **how participants felt about the training** — their satisfaction, engagement, and perception of the program's relevance, content, and delivery.

**Why it is the easiest to evaluate:**
- Data is collected **immediately after training** through simple feedback forms.
- It involves **no long-term observation** or behavioral tracking.
- It is **cost-effective** and requires **minimal expertise** in analytics.
- Responses are **subjective ratings** (e.g., on a 1–5 scale) that can be quantified easily.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students write that "Reaction Level is the most important level." This is **incorrect**. While easy to evaluate, it is the **least valid** indicator of training effectiveness. The KTU board specifically penalizes students who confuse "easy" with "important."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3]**

**(a) Explain in detail Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model of training evaluation. Highlight the tools used at each level.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Model, developed by **Donald L. Kirkpatrick in 1959**, is the most widely used framework for evaluating training programs. It evaluates training at four progressive levels:

**Level 1 – Reaction:**
- **Objective:** To measure how participants **felt** about the training.
- **Tools:** Feedback forms, smile sheets, post-training surveys, focus group discussions.
- **Focus Areas:** Content relevance, trainer effectiveness, training environment, pace of delivery.

**Level 2 – Learning:**
- **Objective:** To measure the **knowledge, skills, and attitudes** acquired.
- **Tools:** Pre-tests and post-tests, written exams, role-plays, case study analysis, skill demonstrations.
- **Focus Areas:** Improvement in test scores, mastery of concepts, confidence in applying learning.

**Level 3 – Behavior:**
- **Objective:** To measure the **transfer of learning to the workplace**.
- **Tools:** On-the-job observation, supervisor reports, 360-degree feedback, performance appraisals.
- **Focus Areas:** Change in work habits, application of new skills, peer and manager observations.

**Level 4 – Results:**
- **Objective:** To measure the **final business/organizational impact**.
- **Tools:** KPIs, balanced scorecards, productivity reports, quality metrics, financial statements.
- **Focus Areas:** Increase in sales, reduction in defects, lower turnover, higher customer satisfaction.

**Significance of the Model:**
- Provides a **structured and logical** approach to evaluation.
- Allows HR professionals to **link training to business outcomes**.
- Helps in **justifying the training budget** to top management.

**(b) Discuss the challenges faced by organizations in evaluating training programs. How can these challenges be overcome?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Training evaluation is critical, but organizations face several challenges:

**1. Difficulty in Isolating Training Effects:**
Training outcomes are often **intermingled with other organizational variables** such as new equipment, motivation programs, or market conditions.
*Solution:* Use **control groups**, **trend analysis**, and **expert estimation** to isolate the training effect.

**2. Time Lag Between Training and Results:**
Behavioral and business results may take **months or years** to manifest.
*Solution:* Conduct **longitudinal studies** and **periodic follow-ups** at 3, 6, and 12 months.

**3. Cost and Complexity of Higher-Level Evaluation:**
Levels 3 and 4 require **significant time, money, and expertise**.
*Solution:* Use **Phillips' ROI methodology** systematically and prioritize evaluation based on **training cost and strategic importance**.

**4. Resistance from Employees and Managers:**
Employees may fear that evaluation will expose their weaknesses, and managers may view it as additional workload.
*Solution:* Build a **culture of learning**, ensure **confidentiality**, and **communicate the benefits** of evaluation to all stakeholders.

**5. Lack of Baseline Data:**
Many organizations **do not collect pre-training performance data**, making it impossible to measure improvement.
*Solution:* Establish **baseline KPIs** before initiating any training program.

**6. Inadequate HR Expertise:**
Many HR teams **lack the analytical skills** required for advanced evaluation.
*Solution:* Invest in **HR analytics training**, hire **specialists**, or use **external consultants** for complex evaluations.

**7. Confidentiality Concerns:**
Detailed evaluation reports may **reveal sensitive business information**.
*Solution:** Use **anonymized data**, share only **summary reports** with external parties, and follow strict **data privacy protocols**.

**Conclusion:** A well-designed evaluation system that balances **cost, time, and analytical rigor** is essential for continuous improvement of training programs.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

**(a) Explain Phillips' Five-Level ROI Model of training evaluation. How is it different from Kirkpatrick's model?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Phillips' Five-Level ROI Model**, developed by **Jack J. Phillips in 1996**, is an **extension of Kirkpatrick's model** that adds a fifth level focused on the **monetary return** of training.

**The Five Levels:**

| Level | Focus | Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Reaction & Satisfaction | Feedback forms, smile sheets |
| **Level 2** | Learning | Pre-tests, post-tests, assessments |
| **Level 3** | Behavior / Application | On-the-job observation, 360° feedback |
| **Level 4** | Business Results | KPIs, productivity, quality, cost |
| **Level 5** | Return on Investment | Monetary value vs. cost (%) |

**Phillips' ROI Process (Five Steps):**
1. Collect post-training reaction data.
2. Confirm learning has occurred.
3. **Isolate training effects** from other organizational factors.
4. **Convert data to monetary value** (e.g., productivity gain × unit cost).
5. **Calculate ROI** and communicate to stakeholders.

**Differences from Kirkpatrick's Model:**

| Aspect | Kirkpatrick | Phillips |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Levels** | Four | Five (adds ROI) |
| **Monetary Focus** | Optional in Level 4 | Central (Level 5) |
| **Isolation of Effects** | Not explicitly addressed | Explicit step in ROI process |
| **Audience** | HR and training managers | Senior executives and CFOs |
| **Data Conversion** | Qualitative and quantitative | Monetary values emphasized |
| **Strategic Value** | Tactical | Strategic (budget justification) |

**Conclusion:** Phillips' model is **more comprehensive** and **executive-friendly**, making it ideal for justifying training investments in business terms.

**(b) A company conducted a 3-day sales training program for 40 sales executives. The total cost of the program was INR 4,00,000. After training, the monthly sales per executive increased by INR 25,000 on average. Assuming the benefits sustain for 12 months, calculate the ROI of the training program.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Number of trainees (N) = 40
- Total training cost = INR 4,00,000
- Monthly sales increase per executive = INR 25,000
- Duration of benefit = 12 months

**Step 1: Calculate Total Monetary Benefits**

$$
Total\ Benefit\ =\ N\ \times\ Monthly\ Increase\ \times\ 12
$$

$$
Total\ Benefit\ =\ 40\ \times\ 25{,}000\ \times\ 12
$$

$$
Total\ Benefit\ =\ INR\ 1{,}20{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Net Program Benefits**

$$
Net\ Benefit\ =\ Total\ Benefit\ -\ Total\ Cost
$$

$$
Net\ Benefit\ =\ 1{,}20{,}00{,}000\ -\ 4{,}00{,}000\ =\ INR\ 1{,}16{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Calculate ROI**

$$
ROI\ (\%)\ =\ \frac{Net\ Benefit}{Total\ Cost}\ \times\ 100
$$

$$
ROI\ (\%)\ =\ \frac{1{,}16{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000}\ \times\ 100
$$

$$
ROI\ (\%)\ =\ 29\ \times\ 100\ =\ \mathbf{2900\%}
$$

**Interpretation:**
The training program generated an ROI of **2900%**, meaning every INR 1 spent on training produced INR 29 in net returns. This is an **exceptionally high ROI**, making the program highly successful from a business perspective.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 12 months** — many students calculate ROI based on a single month's benefit, which is incorrect.
> 2. **Not subtracting program cost** from total benefits before computing ROI — this is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing "2900 times" instead of "2900%"** — the unit must be expressed as a **percentage**.
> 4. **Not providing an interpretation** — the KTU board awards 1 mark for a meaningful business interpretation of the result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam**

- **Definition:** Training evaluation is the systematic assessment of training effectiveness at multiple levels.
- **Kirkpatrick's Model** has **4 levels**: Reaction → Learning → Behavior → Results.
- **Phillips' Model** has **5 levels**, adding **ROI** at the top.
- **Level 1 is the easiest but least valid**; **Level 4 is the hardest but most valuable**.
- **ROI Formula:** $ROI\ (\%)\ =\ \dfrac{Net\ Benefits}{Program\ Costs}\ \times\ 100$
- **Net Benefits** = Total Monetary Benefits − Program Costs.
- **Always express ROI in percentage** with a **business interpretation**.
- **CIRO Model** = Context, Input, Reaction, Output.
- **CIPP Model** = Context, Input, Process, Product.
- **Kaufman** adds **Mega Level** (societal impact) above Level 4.
- **Brinkerhoff's Success Case Method** focuses on **best and worst** cases.
- **Common challenges:** isolating effects, time lag, cost, resistance, lack of baseline data.
- **Tools at Level 1:** Smile sheets, surveys.
- **Tools at Level 2:** Pre-test, post-test, skill assessment.
- **Tools at Level 3:** Observation, 360° feedback, supervisor reports.
- **Tools at Level 4:** KPIs, balanced scorecard, financial reports.
- **Tools at Level 5:** Cost-benefit analysis, ROI percentage.
- **Engineering relevance:** ISO 9001, safety training, Six Sigma, software upskilling.
- **Remember:** ROI is **NOT** the only goal — qualitative outcomes also matter.
- **For 14-mark questions:** Always present **theory + diagram + example + challenges + conclusion**.
- **For 3-mark questions:** Keep answers **crisp, definition-first, and include examples**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In Part B questions, always draw a **labeled diagram** (Kirkpatrick or Phillips pyramid) using either Mermaid-style description or a textual pyramid. Examiners award **2–3 extra marks** for well-labeled visual representations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Past Oriented and Future Oriented Employee Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Past Oriented and Future Oriented Employee Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Past Oriented Training** (also called *Traditional* or *Remedial Training*) is a category of employee learning interventions that focuses on **rectifying existing skill deficiencies**, correcting present performance gaps, and reinforcing current job-specific competencies. It is essentially a **reactive, corrective, and present-focused** approach to learning and development (L&D).

**Future Oriented Training** (also called *Forward-Looking*, *Anticipatory*, or *Strategic Training*) is a category of learning interventions designed to **prepare employees for upcoming challenges**, emerging technologies, evolving job roles, and long-term organizational objectives. It is fundamentally a **proactive, preventive, and future-driven** approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The dichotomy between Past Oriented and Future Oriented training is central to Module 3. Examiners frequently frame questions asking students to **compare, contrast, or justify** the suitability of each approach in a given business scenario. Mastering this distinction is critical for securing high marks in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor treating a patient**:

- **Past Oriented Training** is like giving **medicine to cure an existing illness**. The patient is already sick (current performance gap), and the doctor prescribes treatment to fix the problem right now. The focus is on what is *wrong today*.
- **Future Oriented Training** is like giving **vaccines to prevent future illnesses**. The patient is healthy now, but the doctor anticipates future health risks (technological disruption, market change) and inoculates them with the right skills to stay healthy tomorrow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for B.Tech Students:**
> Think of your KTU semester exams. *Past Oriented Training* is like revising yesterday's syllabus units to fix your weak topics before the exam. *Future Oriented Training* is like learning Python, AI/ML, or Cloud Computing today to stay employable 5 years from now — even though your current syllabus doesn't demand it.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Training** | A short-term, job-specific learning process aimed at developing particular skills |
| **Development** | A long-term, knowledge-based learning process aimed at overall growth |
| **Remedial Training** | Corrective training given when performance falls below acceptable standards |
| **Foresight Training** | Anticipatory training that prepares workers for future job demands |
| **OJT (On-the-Job Training)** | Learning while performing the actual job under supervision |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *PAST = PROBLEM now, FIX now.* *FUTURE = ANTICIPATE tomorrow, PREPARE today.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Past Oriented Training — Deep Dive

Past Oriented Training is grounded in the philosophy that **employee performance must first meet current standards before future aspirations can be pursued**. It is tightly coupled with the **performance appraisal cycle**.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets):**

- **Step 1 — Performance Gap Identification:** The supervisor or HR identifies that an employee's current performance is below the expected standard (via appraisal, feedback, or customer complaints).
- **Step 2 — Root Cause Analysis:** Determine whether the gap is due to **lack of skill, lack of knowledge, or lack of motivation**. Training is the solution for the first two causes only.
- **Step 3 — Training Needs Analysis (TNA):** A formal TNA is conducted at the **organizational, task, and person level** to pinpoint exactly what training is needed.
- **Step 4 — Design and Delivery:** A training program is designed (objectives, content, methods, duration) and delivered using traditional methods such as **lectures, demonstrations, OJT, coaching**.
- **Step 5 — Evaluation:** The Kirkpatrick Model (Reaction → Learning → Behavior → Results) is applied to measure the effectiveness of training.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is called "Past Oriented":** Because the *trigger* for training is always a **past or current deficiency** — something that has already gone wrong or is currently underperforming.

**Common Methods of Past Oriented Training:**

- **On-the-Job Training (OJT):** Learning at the actual workstation under a senior worker or supervisor.
- **Job Rotation:** Moving employees across different departments to build multi-skill capability in their *current* job family.
- **Coaching:** One-on-one guidance from a senior to correct specific weaknesses.
- **Mentoring:** Long-term advisory relationship for professional growth within the current role.
- **Apprenticeship Programs:** Structured learn-while-you-work programs for trades and crafts.
- **Vestibule Training:** Training on simulated equipment in a separate room before actual production.
- **Classroom Lectures / Workshops:** Instructor-led sessions on technical/operational topics.
- **Refresher Courses:** Re-training on previously learned skills to combat skill decay.

### 2.2 Future Oriented Training — Deep Dive

Future Oriented Training operates on the premise that **change is constant** and organizations must build *antifragile* workforces capable of adapting to **technological, economic, and social disruptions**.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets):**

- **Step 1 — Environmental Scanning:** HR conducts **PESTLE analysis** (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental) and **SWOT analysis** to anticipate future business scenarios.
- **Step 2 — Forecasting Skill Demand:** Use tools like **Delphi technique, scenario planning, and competency mapping** to predict which skills will be required 3–10 years ahead.
- **Step 3 — Competency Mapping:** Identify the *future competency gap* between current workforce capabilities and anticipated requirements.
- **Step 4 — Strategic L&D Intervention:** Design programs that build *future-ready* skills — digital literacy, AI/ML awareness, design thinking, leadership pipelines, cross-cultural competence.
- **Step 5 — Continuous Reskilling & Upskilling:** Embed learning as a continuous process to ensure the workforce never becomes obsolete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is called "Future Oriented":** Because the *driver* for training is **anticipated change** — what *will* be needed, not what *is* missing today.

**Common Methods of Future Oriented Training:**

- **Foresight Training:** Explicitly teaches employees to anticipate and prepare for future scenarios.
- **Simulation-Based Training:** VR/AR or computer-based simulations of future work scenarios.
- **Cross-Functional / Multitasking Training:** Preparing employees for roles outside their current function.
- **Action Learning:** Solving real organizational problems in teams to develop leadership and strategic thinking.
- **Scenario Planning Workshops:** Teams work through hypothetical future business scenarios.
- **Leadership Development Programs (LDPs):** High-potential employees groomed for future senior roles.
- **Digital and AI Skill Training:** Preparing for Industry 4.0, automation, and data-driven decision-making.
- **Strategic HR Development:** Building a future-ready talent pipeline aligned with long-term business strategy.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Critical for Exams)

| Dimension | Past Oriented Training | Future Oriented Training |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Focus** | Present and immediate past | Future (3–10 years ahead) |
| **Primary Purpose** | Corrective / Remedial | Preventive / Developmental |
| **Trigger** | Current performance gap | Anticipated future change |
| **Approach** | Reactive | Proactive |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term | Long-term |
| **Risk Mitigation** | Fixes existing weaknesses | Prevents future obsolescence |
| **Link to Appraisal** | Strong (driven by performance reviews) | Weak (driven by strategy) |
| **Typical Methods** | OJT, Coaching, Lectures, Refresher | Simulations, Foresight, Cross-functional, LDPs |
| **Cost Profile** | Lower initial cost, recurring | Higher upfront cost, strategic ROI |
| **KTU Examples** | Banking teller re-trained on new software | Bank staff trained on blockchain, AI chatbots before launch |
| **Bloom's Level** | Apply / Analyze | Evaluate / Create |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a 14-mark question asks for a comparison, students often lose marks by listing features *without contrast*. Always use a **two-column comparison table** in your answer sheet — examiners explicitly reward structured presentation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **manufacturing firms**, past oriented training is used when a CNC operator's output defects rise (retraining on machine settings).
- In the **same firm**, future oriented training is used to upskill the workforce on **collaborative robots (cobots), IoT sensors, and predictive maintenance** before the factory becomes a *smart factory*.
- In **IT services companies** (major KTU recruiters like TCS, Infosys, Wipro), past oriented training covers current stack defects, while future oriented training covers GenAI, quantum computing, and cloud-native architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivation

### 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Training

The following matrix is a step-by-step logical derivation for selecting the appropriate training orientation in a real KTU case-study scenario.

**Decision Flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify the Performance Trigger (P)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Classify Trigger as REACTIVE (R) or ANTICIPATORY (A)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Apply Decision Rule:} \\
& \text{IF } P = R \rightarrow \text{Choose Past Oriented Training} \\
& \text{IF } P = A \rightarrow \text{Choose Future Oriented Training} \\
& \text{IF } P = R + A \rightarrow \text{Choose Blended Approach}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Case Study Style)

**Case:** *A leading KTU-recruited IT firm in Technopark, Trivandrum, notices that its 500 Java developers are delivering below the expected sprint velocity. Simultaneously, the CEO wants the firm to pivot to AI/ML services within 2 years.*

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Problem 1:} \quad & \text{Low current velocity in Java} \\
\text{Classification:} \quad & \text{REACTIVE (existing gap)} \\
\text{Solution:} \quad & \text{PAST ORIENTED Training — Refresher on Java, code review coaching, OJT} \\
\\
\text{Problem 2:} \quad & \text{Need to pivot to AI/ML in 2 years} \\
\text{Classification:} \quad & \text{ANTICIPATORY (future need)} \\
\text{Solution:} \quad & \text{FUTURE ORIENTED Training — Python, TensorFlow, GenAI bootcamps, cross-functional projects}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Strategic Recommendation:** Adopt a **Blended Training Architecture** — combining both orientations to fix today's performance and build tomorrow's capability simultaneously.

### 3.3 Cost-Benefit Analytical Framework

Let us derive a simple analytical expression for training ROI applicable to both orientations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Training ROI (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Training Benefits} - \text{Training Costs}}{\text{Training Costs}} \times 100 \\
\\
\text{Where:} \quad & \text{Training Benefits} = \text{Productivity Gain} + \text{Quality Improvement} + \text{Error Reduction} \\
& \text{Training Costs} = \text{Direct Costs} + \text{Indirect Costs} + \text{Opportunity Cost}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- **Past Oriented Training ROI** tends to be **measurable in the short term** (3–6 months) because benefits like reduced errors and improved throughput are tangible and immediate.
- **Future Oriented Training ROI** is often **measurable in the long term** (1–3 years) because benefits like innovation capacity and strategic agility accrue slowly.

### 3.4 Strategic Blending Equation

A modern KTU-2024 scheme answer should always advocate a **blended approach**. This can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Total}} &= \alpha \cdot T_{\text{Past}} + \beta \cdot T_{\text{Future}} \\
\\
\text{Where:} \quad & \alpha + \beta = 1 \\
& \alpha \in [0.3, 0.6] \text{ (recommended weight for past oriented, since firms must fix today's business)} \\
& \beta \in [0.4, 0.7] \text{ (recommended weight for future oriented, as competitive survival demands)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always recommend a **60:40 or 50:50 ratio** favouring future oriented training in your final answers. Examiners reward forward-thinking strategic answers in NEP 2020-aligned questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Employee Training Orientation Architecture

```mermaid

    A[Employee Training System] --> B[Past Oriented Training]
    A --> C[Future Oriented Training]

    I --> K
    J --> K

    L --> M[Skill Forecasting]

    N --> O[Methods Selection]
    O --> P[Foresight Training]
    O --> Q[Simulation Based Training]
    O --> R[Cross Functional Training]
    O --> S[Leadership Development Programs]
    P --> T[Strategic Readiness]
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
    K --> U[Blended Strategic Outcome]
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow Diagram — Choosing the Right Training Orientation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Identify Training Trigger] --> B{Trigger is Present or Future}
    B -->|Present Performance Gap| C[Past Oriented Training]
    B -->|Anticipated Future Change| D[Future Oriented Training]
    B -->|Both Present and Future| E[Blended Training Approach]
    C --> F[Apply Reactive Methods like OJT, Coaching, Refresher]
    D --> G[Apply Proactive Methods like Foresight, Simulations, LDPs]
    E --> H[Combine Reactive and Proactive Methods]
    F --> I[Short Term Performance Fix]
```
