4. **Poor Implementation:** Many establishments (especially in the unorganized sector) **never constitute** Works Committees despite legal mandate.
5. **Top-Down Nomination:** In practice, employer nominees often dominate the agenda, undermining genuine bipartism.

**Effectiveness of Tripartite Mechanisms:**

**Strengths:**
1. **National-Level Coordination:** The ILC and SLC provide a **macro-level platform** for policy deliberation — the ILC has shaped landmark legislation (Factories Act amendments, Minimum Wages Act, social security codes).
2. **Binding Adjudication:** Industrial Tribunals ensure **enforceable awards** — the ultimate guarantee against employer or worker unilateralism.
3. **Wage Standardization:** Wage Boards have fixed sectoral minimum wages (e.g., **journalists, mine workers**) that prevent race-to-the-bottom wage competition.
4. **Neutral Adjudication:** The Presiding Officer of a Tribunal is a judicial figure, ensuring **fair hearing** for both parties.

**Limitations:**
1. **Advisory Nature of ILC/SLC:** The ILC's recommendations are **not binding** — governments often ignore them for political expediency.
2. **Case Backlog:** Industrial Tribunals are notoriously **overburdened** — disputes take 5–10 years for resolution, defeating the purpose.
3. **Government Bias:** Critics argue that the government, while supposedly neutral, often **legislates in favour of employers** to attract investment — compromising true tripartism.
4. **Declining Frequency:** The ILC has met only **15–20 times in 80+ years** — its role as a vibrant forum has diminished.
5. **Fragmented Trade Unionism:** India's trade union landscape is **highly fragmented** (over 12 central trade unions), making consensus-building difficult.

**Conclusion:** Bipartite mechanisms are **essential for shop-floor harmony** but lack enforcement power. Tripartite mechanisms provide **macro-level policy guidance and binding adjudication** but suffer from procedural delays and political interference. The **2020 Labour Codes** attempt to streamline tripartite consultation by constituting **State and National Labour Commissions** — but the **real success of bipartism and tripartism depends on political will, employer-worker trust, and enforcement capacity**, not merely on the existence of these bodies.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision):**

- [ ] **Bipartite = 2 parties** (employer + employee) — **no government** as member.
- [ ] **Tripartite = 3 parties** (employer + employee + government).
- [ ] **Works Committee = Section 3, IDA 1947** — applies to establishments with **100+ workmen**.
- [ ] **Tenure of Works Committee = 2 years** — members elected by **secret ballot** (workmen side).
- [ ] **Scope of Works Committee = amity, good relations, common interest** — **NOT** terms of employment already fixed.
- [ ] **ILC = 1942** — apex tripartite body — chaired by **Union Minister of Labour** — **advisory**.
- [ ] **SLC = 1942** — executive arm of ILC — meets more frequently.
- [ ] **Conciliation Officer = Section 4, IDA 1947** — first tripartite intervention.
- [ ] **Board of Conciliation = Section 5, IDA 1947**.
- [ ] **Court of Inquiry = Section 6, IDA 1947** — fact-finding.
- [ ] **Labour Court = Section 7, IDA 1947** — specific matters (retrenchment, discharge).
- [ ] **Industrial Tribunal = Section 7-A, IDA 1947** — adjudicatory, **binding award**, equivalent to Civil Court powers.
- [ ] **National Tribunal = Section 7-B, IDA 1947** — disputes of national importance.
- [ ] **Escalation chain:** Grievance → Works Committee (Bipartite) → Conciliation Officer (Tripartite entry) → Board/Court of Inquiry → Labour Court/Tribunal (Binding).
- [ ] **JMC** = Joint Management Council — voluntary bipartite body for participative management.
- [ ] **Wage Boards** = statutory tripartite bodies for sectoral wage fixation.
- [ ] **ILO Tripartite Declaration 1977** = international backing for tripartism.
- [ ] **ILO Conventions 87 and 98** = basis for bipartite collective bargaining rights.
- [ ] **2020 Labour Codes** = modernized tripartite consultation via State and National Labour Commissions.
- [ ] **Key Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **100 workmen** (Works Committee threshold) with **50 workmen** (Layoff threshold under IDA 1947 Section 25-M).
- [ ] **Key Pitfall:** ILC is **advisory**, not binding. Industrial Tribunal **is binding**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Disputes Act
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Industrial Disputes Act # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Disputes Act, 1947** is the principal legislation enacted by the Government of India for the **investigation, prevention, and settlement of industrial disputes** and to provide for certain safeguards to the workers employed in industries. It extends to the whole of India and came into force on **1st April 1947**. The Act is often called the **"Industrial Constitution of India"** because it provides the legal framework for harmonious industrial relations by balancing the interests of employers, employees, and the State. **Long Title of the Act:** *"An Act to make provision for the prevention and settlement of industrial disputes and for certain other purposes."* ### Key Objectives of the Act (KTU High-Yield) 1. To **prevent** industrial disputes through promotion of good industrial relations. 2. To **investigate** and **settle** disputes that have already arisen. 3. To secure social justice, economic justice, and industrial peace. 4. To provide relief to workmen in matters of lay-off, retrenchment, and closure. 5. To establish **machinery for dispute resolution** (Works Committees, Conciliation Officers, Labour Courts, Industrial Tribunals, etc.). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of the Act as a "Family Court for the Workplace."** Imagine two siblings (Employer and Employee) living in the same house (Industry) and they keep fighting. The family court does NOT throw one of them out — instead, it appoints a **mediator** (Conciliation Officer), then a **judge** (Labour Court / Tribunal) to hear both sides, and gives a **fair verdict** that both must follow. The Industrial Disputes Act is exactly that — a court system **dedicated** to keeping the "work-family" together and peaceful. ## 1.3 Key Definitions (Section 2 of the Act) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following definitions — they appear in every KTU question paper.** | Term | Definition under the Act | |------|--------------------------| | **Industry** | Any systematic activity carried on by cooperation between employer and workmen for the production, supply, or distribution of goods or services with a view to satisfy human wants. (Excludes agriculture, hospitals, educational institutions per State amendments) | | **Workman** | Any person (including apprentice) employed in any industry to do manual, unskilled, skilled, technical, operational, clerical, or supervisory work for hire or reward. **Excludes** those employed in a managerial or administrative capacity, or in a supervisory capacity drawing wages ≥ **₹10,000/month** (originally ₹500). | | **Industrial Dispute** | Any dispute or difference between employers and employers, or between employers and workmen, or between workmen and workmen, which is **connected with** the terms of employment or the conditions of labour. **Note:** An individual workman alone cannot raise a dispute under the Act (post the 2014 amendment, even a single workman can in certain cases). | | **Strike** | A cessation of work by a body of persons employed in any industry acting in combination, or a concerted refusal, or a refusal under a common understanding, of any number of persons who are or have been so employed to continue to work or to accept employment. | | **Lock-out** | The temporary closing of a place of employment, or the suspension of work, or the refusal by an employer to continue to employ any number of persons employed by him. | | **Lay-off** | The failure, refusal, or inability of an employer to give employment to a workman whose name is on the muster rolls. | | **Retrenchment** | Termination by the employer of the service of a workman for any reason whatsoever, **except** as a punishment inflicted by way of disciplinary action, voluntary retirement, retirement on reaching superannuation, or termination on account of non-renewal of contract. | | **Closure** | The permanent closing down of a place of employment or part thereof. | ## 1.4 Scope and Applicability > [!NOTE] > - The Act applies to the **whole of India**. > - It applies to all industries, including **public sector** undertakings. > - It does **not** apply to agricultural operations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Machinery for Settlement of Industrial Disputes (Chapter II — Sections 3–21) The Act establishes a **three-tiered administrative and judicial machinery** for dispute resolution. This is the **most important KTU topic** under this Act. ### Tier 1: Prevention Machinery (Mediation Level) > [!IMPORTANT] > **A. Works Committee — Section 3** > - **Purpose:** To promote **good relations** between employer and workmen. > - **Composition:** Equal number of representatives of employer and workmen (not exceeding **20 members** total). > - **Applicability:** Establishments employing **100 or more workmen**. > - **Function:** Discuss matters of common interest, settle minor grievances, and foster cooperation. > - **Key Point:** It is a **consultative** body — it does **NOT** have adjudication powers. > [!NOTE] > **B. Conciliation Officers — Section 4** > - Appointed by the appropriate Government. > - **Duties:** Mediate, promote amicable settlement, investigate the dispute, and submit a **Failure Report (FR)** if settlement fails within 14 days. > - **Powers:** May require parties to attend conciliation proceedings and may inspect records. > [!IMPORTANT] > **C. Board of Conciliation — Section 5** > - Constituted by the appropriate Government for **specific disputes**. > - **Composition:** A **Chairman** and **2 or 4 members** representing parties equally. > - **Function:** Conciliate and promote settlement of disputes. > - **Time Limit:** Board must submit its report within **2 months** (Section 13). ### Tier 2: Investigation Machinery > [!NOTE] > **Court of Inquiry — Section 6** > - Constituted by the appropriate Government to **investigate** and report on: > 1. Any matter connected with or relevant to an industrial dispute. > 2. Any matter referred to it by the appropriate Government. > - **Composition:** One or more persons as the Court. > - **Output:** A **public report** submitted to the Government. > - **Key Feature:** It does **NOT adjudicate** — only **investigates** and reports. ### Tier 3: Adjudication Machinery (Quasi-Judicial) > [!IMPORTANT] > **A. Labour Court — Section 7** > - Established by the appropriate Government. > - **Composition:** A **single person** (Presiding Officer) who is a **qualified judge** or has been a judicial officer. > - **Jurisdiction:** Adjudicates disputes relating to: > - **Rights** (existing legal rights of workmen) — e.g., non-payment of wages, wrongful dismissal. > - Matters specified in the **Third Schedule** (employment, leave, holidays, etc.). > - Discharge or dismissal of workmen not in violation of Standing Orders. > - **Award:** Binding on parties. > [!IMPORTANT] > **B. Industrial Tribunal — Section 7A** > - Established by the appropriate Government. > - **Composition:** A **single person** (Presiding Officer) or a **Bench**. > - **Jurisdiction:** Adjudicates disputes relating to: > - **Interest disputes** (e.g., wage revision, bonus, new terms). > - Matters in the **Second Schedule** (wages, hours, bonus, rationalization, etc.). > - Any dispute referred to it. > - **Award:** Binding on all parties to whom it applies. > [!NOTE] > **C. National Industrial Tribunal — Section 7B** > - Established by the **Central Government** for disputes of **national importance** or affecting multiple states. > - Adjudicates disputes referred by the Central Government. > - Its award is **binding on all establishments** to which it applies throughout India. ## 2.2 The Settlement Hierarchy (Formula Sheet) | Type of Settlement | Binding On | Notes | |--------------------|------------|-------| | **Settlement reached in conciliation proceedings** (Section 2(p)) | All parties to the dispute | Valid for the period agreed, max **6 months** if not fixed | | **Settlement arrived at outside conciliation** (Section 2(p)) | Only those who signed it | Not binding on non-signatories | | **Award of Labour Court / Tribunal** | Parties to the dispute + others in similar establishments as notified | Binding for the period fixed (max 1 year) | | **Settlement vs. Award** | Settlement > Award under Section 18(2) | **Most Important KTU point** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A settlement arrived at in the course of conciliation proceedings is binding on **all parties to the dispute**, not just signatories. But a settlement outside conciliation binds only the signatories. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Strikes & Lockouts | Condition | Strike | Lock-out | |-----------|--------|----------| | **Notice period required** | **6 weeks** (Section 22) | **6 weeks** (Section 22) | | **When prohibited** | During conciliation/arbitration/tribunal proceedings + 7 days thereafter | Same as strike | | **In public utility services** | **14 days' notice** mandatory (Section 22) | **14 days' notice** mandatory | | **Who can declare** | A body of workmen acting in combination | Employer | | **Illegality** | Strike commenced or continued in contravention of Sections 22/23 | Lockout declared/proceeded in contravention | ## 2.4 Lay-off, Retrenchment & Closure — Compensation Formula > [!IMPORTANT] > **These are HIGH-WEIGHTAGE KTU numerical questions.** ### Lay-off Compensation (Section 25C) $$\text{Lay-off Compensation} = 50\% \text{ of basic wages + dearness allowance}$$ For the period of lay-off, **excluding** days of lay-off for which compensation is not payable (max **45 days in any period of 12 months**). ### Retrenchment Compensation (Section 25F(b)) $$\text{Retrenchment Compensation} = 15 \text{ days' wages} \times \text{Years of continuous service (or part thereof, subject to max)}$$ Where **"15 days' wages"** means the average of his wages for the **last 3 months** of continuous service. ### Closure Compensation (Section 25F(b) read with 25FFF) $$\text{Closure Compensation} = 15 \text{ days' wages} \times \text{Years of continuous service}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conditions for Retrenchment (Section 25F):** > 1. **3 months' notice** in writing + wages for the notice period, OR > 2. Pay wages in lieu of notice. > 3. **Retrenchment compensation** as computed above. > 4. Notice of retrenchment to the **appropriate Government**. ## 2.5 The 60-Day Rule (Section 25F, Proviso) > [!IMPORTANT] > **For establishments employing 100 or more workmen:** The employer must take **prior permission** of the appropriate Government before retrenchment, lay-off, or closure. This is the famous **Section 25N / 25O** for establishments with **100+ workmen** (Chapter VB). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - For a **Mechanical/Civil engineer** managing a factory: This Act dictates the legal process for **shutting down operations** — closure compensation must be calculated precisely. - For an **HR professional**: The Act is the **backbone** of every disciplinary action, strike handling, and termination in industrial settings. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation — Retrenchment Compensation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU 14-mark numerical problem pattern.** **Problem:** A workman, Mr. X, has been continuously employed for **12 years and 4 months** in an engineering company. His monthly wages for the last 3 months are **₹18,000, ₹19,000, and ₹20,000**. The employer decides to retrench him. Calculate the retrenchment compensation payable. ### Step 1: Identify the Formula $$\text{Retrenchment Compensation} = 15 \text{ days' average wages} \times \text{Years of continuous service}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Average Wages (Last 3 Months) The "15 days' wages" are computed based on the **average monthly wage** for the last 3 months. $$\text{Average Monthly Wage} = \frac{18000 + 19000 + 20000}{3} = \frac{57000}{3} = 19000 \text{ INR}$$ ### Step 3: Convert to Daily Wage A month is taken as **26 working days** (standard convention under the Act). $$\text{Daily Wage} = \frac{19000}{26} = 730.77 \text{ INR per day}$$ ### Step 4: Compute 15 Days' Wages $$\text{15 Days' Wages} = 15 \times 730.77 = 10961.54 \text{ INR}$$ ### Step 5: Calculate Years of Continuous Service Continuous service of **12 years and 4 months** → **last completed year + fraction**. $$\text{Years of Service} = 12 + \frac{4}{12} = 12 + 0.333 = 12.33 \text{ years}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention:** Any **part of a year** beyond 6 months is rounded up; below 6 months may be ignored. For 4 months, we generally count it as a fraction. The conservative approach (and most common in KTU) is **12.33 years** or **13 years** (if the company rounds up to the next year for safety). Let us use **12.33 years** (raw calculation): $$\text{Retrenchment Compensation} = 10961.54 \times 12.33$$ $$\text{Retrenchment Compensation} = 135,154.69 \text{ INR}$$ ### Step 6: Final Result Table | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Average Monthly Wage (last 3 months) | ₹19,000 | | Daily Wage | ₹730.77 | | 15 Days' Wages | ₹10,961.54 | | Years of Service | 12.33 years | | **Total Retrenchment Compensation** | **₹1,35,154.69 (approx)** | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation — Lay-off Compensation **Problem:** Mr. Y, a workman in a manufacturing unit, is laid off for **25 days**. His basic + DA monthly wage is **₹15,000**. Calculate the lay-off compensation. ### Step 1: Identify the Formula $$\text{Lay-off Compensation} = 50\% \text{ of (Basic + DA)} \text{ per day of lay-off}$$ ### Step 2: Compute Daily Wage $$\text{Daily Wage} = \frac{15000}{26} = 576.92 \text{ INR/day}$$ ### Step 3: Compute 50% of Daily Wage $$\text{Lay-off Compensation per day} = 0.50 \times 576.92 = 288.46 \text{ INR/day}$$ ### Step 4: Total Compensation for 25 Days $$\text{Total Lay-off Compensation} = 288.46 \times 25 = 7211.54 \text{ INR}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Lay-off compensation is **only** payable if the lay-off is **not due to any strike, slow-down, or any other dispute**. Also, it is paid only for days of lay-off **other than weekly holidays**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conditions for Valid Retrenchment > [!IMPORTANT] > **Section 25F** lays down **three mandatory conditions** that the employer must follow: | S.No | Condition | Description | KTU Mark Allocation | |------|-----------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Notice** | 3 months' written notice (or wages in lieu thereof) to the workman | 2 marks | | 2 | **Notice to Government** | Notice of retrenchment to the appropriate Government | 1 mark | | 3 | **Compensation** | Retrenchment compensation = 15 days' wages × years of service | 4 marks | **Failure to comply** → **Retrenchment is void** and the workman is entitled to **reinstatement with full back wages**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Adjudicatory Bodies | Feature | Labour Court | Industrial Tribunal | National Industrial Tribunal | |---------|--------------|--------------------|---------------------------------| | **Section** | 7 | 7A | 7B | | **Established by** | Appropriate Govt. | Appropriate Govt. | Central Government | | **Scope** | Disputes of **Rights** | Disputes of **Interest** | Disputes of **National importance** | | **Subject Matter** | Third Schedule matters | Second Schedule matters | Inter-state disputes | | **Tenure of Award** | Max 1 year (extendable) | Max 1 year (extendable) | As specified | | **Award binding on** | Parties + similar establishments | Parties + similar establishments | All across India | ## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Settlement Process (Flow) > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU diagram-based question — 7 to 14 marks.** 1. **Dispute arises** between employer and workmen. 2. Parties approach the **Conciliation Officer** (mandatory in public utility services). 3. Conciliation Officer **mediates** and tries to bring about a settlement. 4. If settlement is reached → **Settlement is binding** on all parties (Section 18). 5. If no settlement within **14 days** → CO submits **Failure Report (FR)** to the Government. 6. Government may: a. Refer to **Board of Conciliation**, OR b. Refer to **Court of Inquiry**, OR c. Refer to **Labour Court / Tribunal / National Tribunal** for adjudication. 7. Adjudicating body hears both sides and issues an **Award**. 8. Award is **binding** on parties to whom it applies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** The **appropriate Government** depends on the industry — Central Government for industries under its control (railways, atomic energy, etc.); State Government for others. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Machinery for Settlement of Industrial Disputes ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Industrial Dispute Arises"] --> B{"Public Utility Service?"} B -- Yes --> C["Conciliation Officer: 14 Days Mediation"] B -- No --> D["Direct Reference by Government"] C --> E{"Settlement Reached?"} E -- Yes --> F["Binding Settlement - Section 18"] E -- No --> G["Failure Report to Government"] D --> H["Government Decides Reference"] G --> H H --> I{"Type of Reference"} I -- Investigation --> J["Court of Inquiry - Section 6"] I -- Mediation --> K["Board of Conciliation - Section 5"] I -- Rights Dispute --> L["Labour Court - Section 7"] I -- Interest Dispute --> M["Industrial Tribunal - Section 7A"] I -- National Importance --> N["National Industrial Tribunal - Section 7B"] L --> O["Binding Award"] M --> O N --> O J --> P["Public Report to Government"] K --> Q{"Settlement?"} Q -- Yes --> F Q -- No --> H ``` ## 4.2 Authorities and Their Functions — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PreventionLevel["PREVENTION LEVEL - Section 3 and 4"] WC["Works Committee
100 plus workmen
Consultative Body"] CO["Conciliation Officer
Mediation
Failure Report within 14 days"] end subgraph InvestigationLevel["INVESTIGATION LEVEL - Section 6"] CI["Court of Inquiry
Investigates and Reports
No Adjudication Power"] end subgraph AdjudicationLevel["ADJUDICATION LEVEL - Sections 7, 7A, 7B"] LC["Labour Court
Rights Disputes
Third Schedule"] IT["Industrial Tribunal
Interest Disputes
Second Schedule"] NIT["National Industrial Tribunal
Disputes of National Importance"] end WC --> CO CO --> CI CO --> LC CO --> IT CO --> NIT ``` ## 4.3 Section 25F Compliance — Retrenchment Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Employer Decides to Retrench"] --> N1["Issue 3 Months Notice in Writing
OR Pay Wages in Lieu"] N1 --> N2["Send Notice to Appropriate Government"] N2 --> N3["Calculate Retrenchment Compensation
15 Days Wages x Years of Service"] N3 --> Comp{"All Three Conditions Met?"} Comp -- Yes --> Valid["Retrenchment is Valid"] Comp -- No --> Void["Retrenchment is ILLEGAL
Reinstatement with Back Wages"] ``` ## 4.4 Strike vs Lockout — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StrikeFlow["STRIKE - Section 22 to 24"] S1["Workmen act in combination"] --> S2["Refusal to continue work"] S2 --> S3{"6 Weeks Notice in
Non-Public Utility?"} S3 -- Yes --> S4["14 Days Notice
Public Utility Service"] S3 -- No --> S5["Illegal Strike
Penalty Applicable"] end subgraph LockoutFlow["LOCK-OUT - Section 22 to 24"] L1["Employer temporarily closes establishment"] --> L2["Suspends work"] L2 --> L3{"6 Weeks Notice in
Non-Public Utility?"} L3 -- Yes --> L4["14 Days Notice
Public Utility Service"] L3 -- No --> L5["Illegal Lockout
Penalty Applicable"] end ``` ## 4.5 Lay-off, Retrenchment, and Closure — Comparative Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph LayoffBlock["LAY-OFF - Section 25C"] L1["Employer unable to provide employment"] --> L2["Pay 50 percent of basic plus DA"] L2 --> L3["Max 45 days in 12 months"] end subgraph RetrenchmentBlock["RETRENCHMENT - Section 25F"] R1["Termination of workman service"] --> R2["3 Months Notice plus Compensation"] R2 --> R3["15 Days Wages x Years of Service"] end subgraph ClosureBlock["CLOSURE - Section 25FFF"] C1["Permanent closing of establishment"] --> C2["90 Days Notice to Government"] C2 --> C3["15 Days Wages x Years of Service"] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` > **Define "Industrial Dispute" under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947. Can an individual workman raise an industrial dispute?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** As per **Section 2(k)** of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, *"Industrial Dispute"* means **any dispute or difference between employers and employers, or between employers and workmen, or between workmen and workmen, which is connected with the employment or non-employment or the terms of employment or with the conditions of labour, of any person**. **Individual Dispute (Post-2014 Amendment):** Originally, an individual workman **could not** raise a dispute. However, by the **Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act, 2010, Section 2A** was inserted, allowing a workman to raise a dispute regarding his own **discharge, dismissal, retrenchment, or termination**. Hence, an individual workman **can** now raise certain disputes (not all). (1 mark) --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` > **Distinguish between "Strike" and "Lock-out" under the Industrial Disputes Act.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | Strike | Lock-out | |-------|--------|----------| | **Initiator** | Workmen act in combination (1 mark) | Employer (1 mark) | | **Meaning** | Cessation of work + refusal to accept employment | Temporary closing of workplace + refusal to employ workmen (1 mark) | | **Purpose** | To enforce a demand or protest | To pressurize workmen or as a counter-measure | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Explain the various authorities for the settlement of industrial disputes under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Calculate the retrenchment compensation payable to a workman with **18 years and 8 months** of continuous service, given that his wages for the last three months were **₹22,000, ₹23,000, and ₹24,000**. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) > The Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 establishes a **three-tier machinery** for the prevention, investigation, and adjudication of industrial disputes: 1. **Works Committee (Section 3):** Promotes good industrial relations. Constituted in establishments with **100 or more workmen**. Has equal representation of employers and workmen. Functions as a **consultative body** with no adjudication powers. **(1 Mark)** 2. **Conciliation Officers (Section 4):** Appointed by the appropriate Government. Duties include **mediating, promoting amicable settlement, investigating the dispute**, and submitting a **Failure Report** if settlement is not reached within **14 days**. **(1 Mark)** 3. **Board of Conciliation (Section 5):** Constituted **ad hoc** for specific disputes. Comprises a Chairman and 2 or 4 members representing both parties. Submits its report within **2 months** (Section 13). **(1 Mark)** 4. **Court of Inquiry (Section 6):** Constituted to **investigate and report** on any matter connected with an industrial dispute. Has **no power to adjudicate** — only investigates and submits a public report. **(1 Mark)** 5. **Labour Court (Section 7):** Adjudicates disputes relating to **rights** of workmen (e.g., wrongful dismissal). Deals with matters in the **Third Schedule**. **(1 Mark)** 6. **Industrial Tribunal (Section 7A):** Adjudicates disputes relating to **interests** (e.g., wage revision). Deals with matters in the **Second Schedule**. **(1 Mark)** 7. **National Industrial Tribunal (Section 7B):** Established by the **Central Government** for disputes of **national importance** or inter-state disputes. Its award is binding throughout India. **(1 Mark)** ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) > **Given:** > - Continuous Service: 18 years and 8 months > - Wages for last 3 months: ₹22,000 + ₹23,000 + ₹24,000 **Step 1: Average Monthly Wage (1 Mark)** $$\text{Average Wage} = \frac{22000 + 23000 + 24000}{3} = \frac{69000}{3} = 23000 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 2: Daily Wage (1 Mark)** $$\text{Daily Wage} = \frac{23000}{26} = 884.62 \text{ INR/day}$$ **Step 3: 15 Days' Wages (1 Mark)** $$15 \text{ Days Wages} = 15 \times 884.62 = 13269.23 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 4: Years of Continuous Service (1 Mark)** $$18 \text{ years } 8 \text{ months} = 18 + \frac{8}{12} = 18.67 \text{ years}$$ **Step 5: Retrenchment Compensation (1 Mark)** $$\text{Compensation} = 13269.23 \times 18.67 = 2,47,716.45 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 6: Final Answer with Conditions Mentioned (2 Marks)** > **The employer must also issue a 3-month notice (or wages in lieu) and notify the appropriate Government under Section 25F.** > **Total Retrenchment Compensation Payable: ₹2,47,716.45 (approx)** --- > **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative):** > **(a)** What is a "Lock-out"? Explain the conditions under which a lock-out is legal and illegal under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A workman is laid off for **35 days** in a textile mill. His basic + DA is **₹13,000 per month**. Calculate the lay-off compensation. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) > **Definition (2 Marks):** As per **Section 2(l)** of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, *"Lock-out"* means the **temporary closing of a place of employment**, or the **suspension of work**, or the **refusal by an employer to continue to employ any number of persons employed by him**. **Conditions for a Legal Lock-out (3 Marks):** 1. The employer must give **6 weeks' notice** before the lock-out in a non-public utility service. 2. In a **public utility service**, **14 days' notice** is mandatory (Section 22). 3. The lock-out must **not** be declared during the pendency of conciliation/arbitration proceedings or **within 7 days** after their conclusion (Section 23). 4. The lock-out must not contravene any **Standing Orders** applicable to the establishment. **Illegal Lock-out (2 Marks):** - A lock-out declared in contravention of Sections 22 or 23 is **illegal**. - Any workman laid off under an illegal lock-out is entitled to **wages for the entire period of lock-out**. - The employer may face **penalties under Section 26**. ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Given:** Lay-off period = 35 days, Basic + DA = ₹13,000/month **Step 1: Daily Wage (2 Marks)** $$\text{Daily Wage} = \frac{13000}{26} = 500 \text{ INR/day}$$ **Step 2: 50% of Daily Wage (1 Mark)** $$\text{Lay-off Compensation per day} = 0.50 \times 500 = 250 \text{ INR/day}$$ **Step 3: Total Compensation for 35 Days (2 Marks)** $$\text{Total Compensation} = 250 \times 35 = 8750 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 4: Conditions & Caveats (2 Marks)** > **Conditions:** > 1. Lay-off must **not** be due to any strike or dispute. > 2. The workman's name must be on the muster rolls. > 3. Compensation is for **all days of lay-off excluding weekly holidays**. > 4. Maximum **45 days** compensation is payable in a period of 12 months. > **Total Lay-off Compensation Payable: ₹8,750** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention 26 working days/month** in wage calculations. Students often use 30 days — this leads to **2-mark loss**. > 2. **Rounding errors** in years of service: 4 months and 8 months both may be rounded differently — always specify the rounding convention. > 3. **Mixing up Labour Court and Industrial Tribunal jurisdictions** — Rights vs. Interest is a classic trap question. > 4. **Forgetting to mention "Failure Report within 14 days"** when discussing Conciliation Officer — direct 2-mark loss. > 5. **Not stating Section numbers** in the answer — KTU examiners **deduct marks** for missing statutory references. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Industrial Disputes Act, 1947** ### Core Definitions (High-Frequency) - **Industry, Workman, Industrial Dispute, Strike, Lock-out, Lay-off, Retrenchment, Closure** — All from Section 2. ### Three-Tier Machinery (Most-Asked) - **Prevention:** Works Committee + Conciliation Officer - **Investigation:** Court of Inquiry - **Adjudication:** Labour Court (Rights) → Industrial Tribunal (Interests) → National Industrial Tribunal (National) ### Critical Numbers to Memorize - **100 workmen** — threshold for Works Committee. - **6 weeks** — strike/lockout notice (non-public utility). - **14 days** — strike/lockout notice (public utility) AND conciliation period. - **2 months** — Board of Conciliation report. - **3 months** — retrenchment notice. - **15 days' wages** × years of service — retrenchment and closure compensation. - **50% of basic + DA** — lay-off compensation. - **45 days** — max lay-off compensation in 12 months. - **90 days** — closure notice to Government. - **₹10,000/month** — wage ceiling for "workman" definition. - **100+ workmen** establishments — Section 25N/25O prior permission rule (Chapter VB). ### KTU High-Yield One-Liners - The Act is called the **"Industrial Constitution of India"**. - A **settlement in conciliation** binds all parties; a **settlement outside** binds only signatories. - **Settlement > Award** under Section 18(2) — this is the hierarchy of industrial peace. - **Section 25F** compliance is mandatory — non-compliance makes retrenchment **void ab initio**. - **Strikes and lockouts are prohibited** during conciliation/tribunal proceedings + 7 days thereafter. - **Lay-off is temporary**; **retrenchment is termination**; **closure is permanent shutdown** — never confuse. ### Common Confusions to Avoid - **Lay-off ≠ Retrenchment ≠ Closure** — all three have different compensation formulas and conditions. - **Works Committee ≠ Trade Union** — Works Committee is set up under the Act; Trade Unions are registered under the Trade Unions Act, 1926. - **Court of Inquiry ≠ Labour Court** — the former only investigates; the latter adjudicates. - **Strike is by workmen; Lock-out is by employer** — both have similar legal treatment but different initiators. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always cite the **Section number** in your answer. A 7-mark question on authorities expects at least **3 statutory references** for full marks. Memorize the **Second Schedule and Third Schedule** topics — they appear in nearly every KTU paper.

topics.title: Factories Act
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET721 - HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factories Act

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Factories Act, 1948** is a comprehensive Indian labour legislation enacted to regulate the working conditions in factories, ensure the safety, health, and welfare of workers, and prevent industrial disputes arising from exploitative labour practices. It is the principal statute governing the **safety, health, and welfare of factory workers** in India and applies to all states and union territories.

The Act replaced the earlier Factories Act of 1934 and was amended in 1976 (to extend its scope to establishments employing **10 or more workers** and engaged in manufacturing processes using power). The Act is administered by the **State Governments** through their Factory Inspectorates.

### 1.2 Key Thresholds for Applicability

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Thresholds for Coverage:**
> - **Manufacturing process with power:** Factory with **10 or more workers** working on any day in the preceding 12 months.
> - **Manufacturing process without power:** Factory with **20 or more workers** working on any day in the preceding 12 months.
> - **Manufacturing process** = making, altering, repairing, ornamenting, finishing, packing, oiling, washing, cleaning, breaking up, demolishing, or otherwise treating/adapt any article or substance with a view to its use, sale, transport, delivery, or disposal.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Factories Act as a "Safety Contract":**
> Imagine a factory as a high-speed highway. The Factories Act acts as the **traffic rulebook** for this highway. Just as traffic rules prevent accidents, set speed limits, ensure proper road conditions, and penalize violations — the Factories Act:
> - Sets **speed limits** (working hours and overtime caps)
> - Provides **guardrails** (safety measures and fencing of machinery)
> - Mandates **rest areas** (welfare facilities like canteens, rest rooms)
> - Appoints **traffic police** (Inspectors and Certifying Surgeons)
> - Imposes **fines** (penalties for non-compliance)
>
> Without this rulebook, the "highway" of industry would become chaotic, dangerous, and exploitative. The Act is essentially a **tripartite social contract** between the **State (government)**, **Capital (employers)**, and **Labour (workers)**.

### 1.4 Key Definitions Under the Act

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Factory** | Any premises including precincts where 10+ (with power) or 20+ (without power) workers are/were working on any day in last 12 months |
| **Worker** | A person employed directly or through any agency in any manufacturing process |
| **Occupier** | The person who has ultimate control over the affairs of the factory |
| **Adult** | A worker who has completed **18 years of age** |
| **Adolescent** | A person who has completed 15 years but not 18 years |
| **Young Person** | A person who is either an adolescent or a child |
| **Manufacturing Process** | Any process for making, altering, repairing, ornamenting, finishing, packing, etc. |
| **Day** | A period of **24 hours** beginning at midnight |
| **Week** | A period of **7 days** beginning at midnight on Saturday night |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Major Chapters/Provisions of the Factories Act, 1948

The Act is divided into **12 Chapters and 120 Sections** with **3 Schedules**. Below is a high-yield analysis:

---

#### **Chapter II: The Inspecting Staff**

- **Section 8**: Appointment of Chief Inspector, Additional Chief Inspector, Joint Chief Inspector, Deputy Chief Inspector, and Inspectors of Factories.
- **Section 9**: Powers of Inspectors — entry, inspection, examination, seizure of documents, and requiring production of licenses.
- **Section 10**: Certifying Surgeons — appointed by State Government to conduct medical examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The Inspector has the power to enter, inspect, and even **prosecute** offenders. They are the "eyes and ears" of the State Government.

---

#### **Chapter III: Health Provisions (Sections 11–20)**

| Section | Provision |
|---------|-----------|
| 11 | **Cleanliness** — effective drainage, dirt-free floors, walls, ceilings |
| 12 | **Disposal of wastes and effluents** |
| 13 | **Ventilation and temperature** — 25°C (if heavy work), cooling by air circulation |
| 14 | **Dust and fume** — effective measures to control inhalation |
| 16 | **Drinking water** — wholesome water at convenient points |
| 17 | **Latrines and urinals** — separate for male and female workers |
| 18 | **Spittoons** — at convenient places |

---

#### **Chapter IV: Safety Provisions (Sections 21–41)** — *Highest KTU Weightage*

| Section | Provision |
|---------|-----------|
| 21 | **Fencing of machinery** — every dangerous part must be securely fenced |
| 22 | **Work on or near machinery in motion** — restricted to trained adult males |
| 23 | **Employment of young persons on dangerous machines** — prohibited |
| 25 | **Precautions in case of fire** — fire extinguishers, adequate exits |
| 26 | **Power to require specifications of defective parts** |
| 27 | **Work in confined spaces** — adequate ventilation, trained supervisors |
| 28 | **Hoists and lifts** — safe mechanical construction, periodic examination |
| 29 | **Lifting machines, chains, ropes** — safe load, regular testing |
| 32 | **Floors, stairs, means of access** — safe construction, no overloading |
| 33 | **Pits, sumps, openings in floors** — securely covered or fenced |
| 36 | **Safety equipment** — provided and maintained for personal safety |
| 40 | **Notice of accidents** — to Inspector within prescribed time |
| 41 | **Notice of certain diseases** — occupational diseases |

---

#### **Chapter V: Welfare Provisions (Sections 42–50)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Welfare Facilities:**
> - **Section 42 (Drinking Water)** — wholesome water supply
> - **Section 43 (First Aid)** — First Aid boxes (one for every 150 workers), accessible and visible
> - **Section 44 (Canteen)** — mandatory in factories employing **250+ workers**
> - **Section 45 (Shelters, Rest Rooms, Lunch Rooms)** — mandatory for **100+ workers**
> - **Section 46 (Crèches)** — mandatory for **30+ female workers**, with adequate accommodation
> - **Section 47 (Welfare Officers)** — mandatory in factories employing **500+ workers**
> - **Section 48 (Safety Officers)** — mandatory in factories employing **1000+ workers** or carrying on hazardous processes

---

#### **Chapter VI: Working Hours of Adults (Sections 51–66)**

This is the **most heavily tested chapter** in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Working Hours Framework:**
>
> | Provision | Limit |
> |-----------|-------|
> | Maximum weekly hours | **48 hours** |
> | Maximum daily hours | **9 hours** |
> | Spread-over (inclusive of rest intervals) | **10.5 hours** |
> | Rest interval (after 5 hours of continuous work) | **At least 30 minutes** |
> | Overtime (per day) | Maximum **2 hours** (NOT 3 as commonly mistaken) |
> | Overtime wage | **Twice the rate of ordinary wages** (Section 59) |
> | Weekly holiday | **One full day** (Section 52) |

---

#### **Chapter VII: Employment of Young Persons (Sections 67–77)**

- **Section 67**: Prohibition of employment of children below **14 years**.
- **Section 68**: Certificate of fitness from Certifying Surgeon for adolescents (15–18 years).
- **Section 69**: Working hours for young persons — **maximum 4.5 hours per day**, no night shifts (between 10 PM and 6 AM).

---

#### **Chapter VIII: Annual Leave with Wages (Sections 78–84)**

> [!TIP]
> **Leave Calculation Formula (for Adults):**
>
> $$\text{Leave earned} = 1 \text{ day for every } 20 \text{ days worked}$$
>
> - For **young persons (15–18 years)**: 1 day for every 15 days worked.
> - Maximum accumulation: **30 days** (Adults) / **40 days** (Young Persons).
> - Leave cannot be claimed for less than **4 days** at a time.

---

#### **Chapter X: Penalties (Sections 92–106)**

| Offense | Penalty |
|---------|---------|
| General contravention | Up to **₹10,000** fine or 3 months imprisonment |
| Continued contravention | Up to **₹1,000 per day** additional fine |
| Violation of Section 67 (child labour) | Up to **₹50,000** fine or 2 years imprisonment |
| Obstructing Inspector | Up to **₹10,000** or 6 months imprisonment |
| False information | Up to **₹10,000** or 6 months imprisonment |

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula/Rule | Application |
|---------|--------------|-------------|
| Working hours (weekly) | **≤ 48 hours** | Maximum per week |
| Working hours (daily) | **≤ 9 hours** | Maximum per day |
| Spread-over | **≤ 10.5 hours** | Total presence including rest |
| Rest interval | **≥ 30 minutes** | After 5 continuous hours |
| Overtime (daily) | **≤ 2 hours** | Beyond 9 hours per day |
| Overtime wage | **2 × normal wage** | Per Section 59 |
| Annual leave (adult) | **1 day / 20 days worked** | Pro-rata basis |
| Annual leave (young) | **1 day / 15 days worked** | Pro-rata basis |
| Leave accumulation cap | **30 days (adult), 40 days (young)** | Carried forward |
| First Aid box | **1 per 150 workers** | Mandatory |
| Canteen threshold | **250+ workers** | Mandatory |
| Creche threshold | **30+ female workers** | Mandatory |
| Welfare Officer | **500+ workers** | Mandatory |
| Safety Officer | **1000+ workers / hazardous** | Mandatory |
| Shift change notice | **4 clear days** | Section 56 |
| Child age prohibition | **< 14 years** | Section 67 |
| Adolescent upper limit | **< 18 years** | Section 67 |
| Night work prohibition | **10 PM – 6 AM** | Women & young persons (since 2016 amendment) |
| **Sundays/Holidays** | **Double wages** | Section 59 |
| Notice of accident | **Within prescribed time** | To Inspector (Section 40) |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in HRM

The Factories Act is the **backbone of industrial compliance** in India. HR managers must:
1. Maintain **attendance and overtime records** for inspection.
2. Conduct **periodic medical examinations** through Certifying Surgeons.
3. Ensure **safety committee meetings** and **safety audits**.
4. File **annual returns** (Form 21) and **half-yearly returns** (Form 22) to the Inspector.
5. Maintain **muster roll, overtime register, leave register, accident register, dangerous occurrence register**.
6. Conduct **mock drills** for fire safety.
7. Display **abstract of the Act, working hours, holidays, and emergency contacts** prominently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Overtime Wage Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> Since the Factories Act is a legal/management topic, "derivations" here refer to **statutory calculations and compliance logic**. Below is a step-by-step worked example.

---

#### **Worked Example 1: Weekly Overtime Calculation**

**Problem:** In a factory, an adult worker has the following work schedule in a week:
- Monday to Friday: 9 hours each day
- Saturday: 8 hours
- Sunday (worked): 4 hours

Calculate the **regular wages, overtime wages, and total wages** if his basic hourly rate is **₹50** and a Sunday holiday is due but he worked on it.

---

**Step 1: Identify Regular Working Hours**

$$\text{Monday to Friday} = 5 \text{ days} \times 9 \text{ hours} = 45 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Saturday} = 8 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Total regular hours} = 45 + 8 = 53 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Identify Excess Hours Beyond 48-Hour Weekly Limit**

$$\text{Maximum weekly hours} = 48 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Excess hours} = 53 - 48 = 5 \text{ hours (overtime)}$$

**Step 3: Identify Sunday Working Hours**

$$\text{Sunday work} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

Since Sunday was meant to be a holiday (Section 52 mandates one full day off per week), working on Sunday attracts **double wages** under Section 59.

**Step 4: Calculate Regular Wages (48 hours)**

$$\text{Regular wages} = 48 \times ₹50 = ₹2{,}400$$

**Step 5: Calculate Overtime Wages (5 hours excess)**

$$\text{Overtime wage rate} = 2 \times ₹50 = ₹100/\text{hour}$$

$$\text{Overtime wages} = 5 \times ₹100 = ₹500$$

**Step 6: Calculate Double Wages for Sunday Work (4 hours)**

$$\text{Sunday wage rate} = 2 \times ₹50 = ₹100/\text{hour}$$

$$\text{Sunday wages} = 4 \times ₹100 = ₹400$$

**Step 7: Calculate Total Wages**

$$\text{Total} = \text{Regular} + \text{Overtime} + \text{Sunday}$$

$$\text{Total} = ₹2{,}400 + ₹500 + ₹400$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Wages} = ₹3{,}300}$$

---

#### **Worked Example 2: Annual Leave Calculation**

**Problem:** An adult worker was employed for **240 days** in a year. He already took **10 days** of leave. How many more days of leave is he entitled to under Section 79?

---

**Step 1: Apply the Leave Formula**

$$\text{Leave earned} = 1 \text{ day per } 20 \text{ days worked}$$

$$\text{Leave earned} = \frac{240}{20} = 12 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Subtract Leave Already Availed**

$$\text{Balance leave} = 12 - 10 = 2 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Balance leave due} = 2 \text{ days}}$$

---

#### **Worked Example 3: Daily Overtime Limit Check**

**Problem:** A factory wants to make a worker work **12 hours** on a Monday and **10 hours** on a Tuesday. Is this legal?

---

**Step 1: Check Daily Overtime Limit**

$$\text{Maximum hours/day} = 9 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Monday excess} = 12 - 9 = 3 \text{ hours (overtime)}$$

**Step 2: Verify Overtime Cap**

> [!WARNING]
> **Overtime cap = 2 hours per day (NOT 3 hours).**
> Even though Section 59 allows overtime, Section 64 caps it at **2 hours per day**.

$$\text{Monday: Violation (3 hours overtime exceeds 2-hour cap)}$$

**Step 3: Check Spread-Over Limit**

$$\text{Spread-over cap} = 10.5 \text{ hours}$$

If 12 working hours are inclusive of rest intervals, spread-over = 12 hours **> 10.5 hours = VIOLATION**.

$$\boxed{\text{Both days are ILLEGAL — violates Section 64 (daily OT cap) and Section 56 (spread-over)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threshold-Based Welfare Obligations

| Number of Workers | Mandatory Welfare Provisions |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Any number** | Cleanliness, ventilation, lighting, drinking water, latrines |
| **30+ female workers** | **Crèche** facility (Section 46) |
| **100+ workers** | **Shelter/Rest Room** (Section 45) |
| **150+ workers** | **1 First Aid Box** per 150 workers (Section 43) |
| **250+ workers** | **Canteen** (Section 44) |
| **500+ workers** | **Welfare Officer** (Section 47) |
| **1000+ workers** (or hazardous) | **Safety Officer** (Section 48) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "30-C, 100-R, 250-C, 500-W, 1000-S" → **Crèche, Rest Room, Canteen, Welfare Officer, Safety Officer**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Penalties Matrix (Sections 92–106)

| Type of Offense | Fine (₹) | Imprisonment |
|-----------------|----------|--------------|
| General contravention | Up to 10,000 | Up to 3 months |
| Continued offense | 1,000/day extra | — |
| Child employment (<14 yrs) | Up to 50,000 | Up to 2 years |
| Failure to maintain register | Up to 5,000 | Up to 2 months |
| Obstructing Inspector | Up to 10,000 | Up to 6 months |
| Wrong information to Inspector | Up to 10,000 | Up to 6 months |
| Owner permitting contravention | Same as worker penalty | Same |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazardous Industries Coverage

The Factories Act 1948, read with the **Manufacture, Storage, and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** and the **Factories (Amendment) Act, 1987**, applies special provisions for hazardous industries:

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazardous process indicators:**
> - Handling of flammable substances (petroleum, chemicals)
> - Toxic substances (lead, mercury, asbestos)
> - Explosive materials
> - Dusts (coal, silica)
> - Welding with radiation exposure
>
> For such factories, the State Government can:
> - Appoint **Site Appraisal Committee**
> - Require **On-Site Emergency Plan**
> - Mandate **Safety Audit every 6 months**
> - Require **Health Records of workers** maintained for **30 years**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Overall Architecture of the Factories Act, 1948

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Factories Act 1948] --> B[Chapter I - Preliminary]
    A --> C[Chapter II - Inspecting Staff]
    A --> D[Chapter III - Health Provisions]
    A --> E[Chapter IV - Safety Provisions]
    A --> F[Chapter V - Welfare Provisions]
    A --> G[Chapter VI - Working Hours of Adults]
    A --> H[Chapter VII - Employment of Young Persons]
    A --> I[Chapter VIII - Annual Leave with Wages]
    A --> J[Chapter IX - Special Provisions]
    A --> K[Chapter X - Penalties]
    A --> L[Chapter XI - Miscellaneous]
    A --> M[Chapter XII - Application and Exceptions]
    
    B --> B1[Definitions: Factory, Worker, Adult, Child]
    
    C --> C1[Chief Inspector]
    C --> C2[Additional Inspector]
    C --> C3[Certifying Surgeon]
    
    D --> D1[Cleanliness]
    D --> D2[Ventilation and Temperature]
    D --> D3[Drinking Water]
    D --> D4[Latrines and Urinals]
    
    E --> E1[Fencing of Machinery]
    E --> E2[Fire Precautions]
    E --> E3[Hoists and Lifts]
    E --> E4[Notice of Accidents]
    E --> E5[Occupational Diseases]
    
    F --> F1[Canteen 250+ workers]
    F --> F2[Crèche 30+ female workers]
    F --> F3[Rest Rooms 100+ workers]
    F --> F4[Welfare Officer 500+ workers]
    F --> F5[Safety Officer 1000+ workers]
    
    G --> G1[Max 9 hours per day]
    G --> G2[Max 48 hours per week]
    G --> G3[Max 2 hours overtime]
    G --> G4[Double wages for overtime]
    G --> G5[Weekly holiday mandatory]
    
    H --> H1[No child below 14 years]
    H --> H2[Adolescent 15 to 18 years]
    H --> H3[No night work for young persons]
    
    I --> I1[1 day per 20 days worked]
    I --> I2[Max accumulation 30 days adults]
    I --> I3[Max accumulation 40 days young]
    
    K --> K1[Fine up to Rs 10000]
    K --> K2[Imprisonment up to 3 months]
    K --> K3[Child labor fine Rs 50000]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Working Hours Compliance Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Shift Start] --> B{Working Hours Counted}
    B --> C[Maximum 9 hours per day]
    C --> D{After 5 hours continuous}
    D -->|Yes| E[Mandatory 30 minute rest]
    D -->|No| F[Continue work]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Daily hours exceed 9}
    G -->|No| H[End of day]
    G -->|Yes| I[Overtime hours]
    I --> J{Max 2 hours overtime}
    J -->|Yes| K[Pay double wages]
    J -->|No| L[VIOLATION Section 64]
    K --> H
    H --> M{Weekly total exceeds 48}
    M -->|No| N[Continue next day]
    M -->|Yes| O[Excess overtime at double wages]
    O --> P{Weekly holiday provided}
    P -->|Yes| N
    P -->|No| Q[VIOLATION Section 52]
    
    style L fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style K fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style O fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Factory" and "Worker" as per the Factories Act, 1948.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Factory** is defined under **Section 2(m)** as any premises including the precincts thereof — (i) whereon **10 or more workers** are working or were working on any day of the preceding 12 months, and in any part of which a **manufacturing process is being carried on** with the aid of power; or (ii) whereon **20 or more workers** are working or were working on any day of the preceding 12 months, and in any part of which a **manufacturing process is being carried on** without the aid of power.

> **Worker** is defined under **Section 2(l)** as a person employed, directly or through any agency (whether for wages or not), in any manufacturing process, or in cleaning any part of machinery or premises used for manufacturing, or in any other kind of work incidental to or connected with the manufacturing process or the subject of the manufacturing process.

> However, it does **not include** a member of the armed forces of the Union, or a person who is subject to the Army Act, Navy Act, or Air Force Act.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition of Factory with thresholds: **1.5 marks**
- Definition of Worker with exclusions: **1.5 marks**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: List any six welfare provisions that an occupier is required to provide under the Factories Act.**

**Model Answer:**

> As per **Chapter V (Sections 42–50)** of the Factories Act, 1948, the following welfare provisions must be provided by the occupier:
>
> 1. **Drinking Water (Section 42)** — wholesome water at convenient points.
> 2. **First Aid Boxes (Section 43)** — one for every 150 workers.
> 3. **Canteen (Section 44)** — mandatory in factories employing **250 or more workers**.
> 4. **Shelters, Rest Rooms, Lunch Rooms (Section 45)** — mandatory for **100 or more workers**.
> 5. **Crèches (Section 46)** — mandatory for **30 or more female workers**.
> 6. **Welfare Officers (Section 47)** — mandatory in factories employing **500 or more workers**.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Any 6 provisions correctly stated with thresholds: **3 marks** (0.5 per provision)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain in detail the various provisions of the Factories Act, 1948 related to:**
**(a) Working hours and overtime of adult workers** **(7 marks)**
**(b) Employment of young persons and annual leave with wages** **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Part (a) — Working Hours and Overtime of Adult Workers (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Factories Act, 1948 contains detailed provisions governing the working hours of adult workers under **Chapter VI (Sections 51–66)**. These provisions are designed to prevent exploitation and ensure work-life balance.

**(i) Weekly Hours (Section 51):** No adult worker shall be required or allowed to work in a factory for more than **48 hours in any week**.

**(ii) Daily Hours (Section 52):** No adult worker shall be required or allowed to work in a factory for more than **9 hours in any day**.

**(iii) Weekly Holiday (Section 52):** Every factory must give its workers **one full day off per week**, which shall be specified in the notice displayed in the factory. If a worker works on the designated holiday, he must be given a compensatory holiday within 30 days.

**(iv) Rest Intervals (Section 55):** No adult worker shall be required to work for more than **5 hours continuously** without a rest interval of at least **30 minutes**.

**(v) Spread-Over (Section 56):** The total spread-over inclusive of work and rest intervals shall **not exceed 10.5 hours** in any day. The Manager must give notice of shift changes **4 clear days in advance**.

**(vi) Overtime (Sections 59 & 64):**
- Where a worker works **beyond 9 hours per day or 48 hours per week**, he shall be paid **wages at twice his ordinary rate** (Section 59).
- The total overtime in any quarter shall **not exceed 50 hours**, and **overtime in any day shall not exceed 2 hours** (Section 64).

**(vii) Double Wages on Weekly Holiday:** If a worker works on a Sunday/holiday without compensatory off, he must be paid **double wages** for the entire work done.

**Marks Allocation:**
- Section 51, 52, 55, 56: **3 marks**
- Section 59, 64 (overtime wages and cap): **2.5 marks**
- Weekly holiday rule + double wages: **1.5 marks**

---

##### **Part (b) — Employment of Young Persons & Annual Leave (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(A) Employment of Young Persons (Chapter VII, Sections 67–77):**

**(i) Prohibition of Child Labour (Section 67):** No child who has **not completed 14 years of age** shall be employed in any factory. Violation attracts fine up to **₹50,000** or imprisonment up to **2 years**.

**(ii) Certificate of Fitness (Section 68):** Adolescents (15–18 years) must obtain a **certificate of fitness** from the Certifying Surgeon before being employed. The certificate is valid for **12 months** and must be renewed.

**(iii) Working Hours of Young Persons (Section 69):** No young person shall be employed for more than **4.5 hours per day**, and must not work during **night (10 PM to 6 AM)**.

**(iv) No Double Employment:** Young persons cannot be employed in two factories on the same day.

---

**(B) Annual Leave with Wages (Chapter VIII, Sections 78–84):**

**(i) Leave Calculation (Section 79):**
- **Adults:** **1 day for every 20 days** worked in the previous year.
- **Young Persons:** **1 day for every 15 days** worked.

**(ii) Accumulation Cap:**
- **Adults:** Maximum **30 days** can be carried forward.
- **Young Persons:** Maximum **40 days** can be carried forward.

**(iii) Minimum Availment:** A worker cannot claim leave for less than **4 days** at a time.

**(iv) Notice and Payment:** Leave must be applied **15 days in advance** for 4+ days of leave, and wages must be paid before the leave begins or within the prescribed time.

**Marks Allocation:**
- Child labour prohibition + Certificate of fitness: **2 marks**
- Young persons' working hours + night prohibition: **1.5 marks**
- Leave formula (Adult & Young): **2 marks**
- Accumulation cap + minimum 4-day rule: **1.5 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the safety and health provisions of the Factories Act, 1948. Highlight the role of the Inspector and Certifying Surgeon.**

---

**Model Answer:**

The safety and health provisions form the **core** of the Factories Act, ensuring worker protection from industrial hazards.

**(A) Safety Provisions (Chapter IV, Sections 21–41):**

| Section | Provision |
|---------|-----------|
| 21 | **Fencing of Machinery** — every moving part of dangerous machinery must be securely fenced. |
| 22 | **Work on Machinery in Motion** — only trained adult males (with proper clothing) can work on/near moving machinery. |
| 25 | **Fire Precautions** — adequate fire-fighting equipment, fire exits, fire drills. |
| 26 | Power to require specifications of defective parts. |
| 28 | **Hoists and Lifts** — safe construction, periodic testing, marked safe working load. |
| 29 | **Lifting Machines, Chains, Ropes** — tested annually by competent person. |
| 32–33 | **Floors, Stairs, Pits, Openings** — safely constructed and fenced. |
| 36 | **Personal Safety Equipment** — helmets, goggles, gloves, respirators provided and maintained. |
| 40 | **Notice of Accidents** — written notice to Inspector within prescribed time. |
| 41 | **Notice of Occupational Diseases** — list of 22 notifiable diseases must be reported. |

---

**(B) Health Provisions (Chapter III, Sections 11–20):**

- **Cleanliness (S.11):** Dirt-free floors, walls; painted every 3 years (whitewash every 6 months for non-painted surfaces).
- **Ventilation & Temperature (S.13):** Effective ventilation; temperature ≤ 25°C in cotton textile, jute, and similar factories.
- **Dust & Fume (S.14):** Effective exhaust systems to control inhalation.
- **Lighting (S.15):** Sufficient and suitable lighting in all workrooms.
- **Drinking Water (S.16):** Wholesome water at convenient points; signposted "Drinking Water".
- **Latrines (S.17):** Separate facilities for male and female workers.
- **Spittoons (S.18):** Provided at convenient places and maintained hygienically.

---

**(C) Role of the Inspector (Section 9):**

The Inspector of Factories is appointed by the **State Government** and has wide-ranging powers:

1. **Right of Entry:** Can enter any factory at any reasonable time.
2. **Examination of Premises:** Can examine buildings, machinery, and records.
3. **Seizure of Documents:** Can seize any register, certificate, or document.
4. **Taking Samples:** Can take samples of substances used in manufacturing.
5. **Requiring Production:** Can demand production of licenses and certificates.
6. **Prosecution:** Can initiate legal proceedings against offenders.
7. **Issuing Orders:** Can direct rectification of defects within a specified time.

> The Inspector is the **primary enforcement arm** of the Act and acts as a bridge between the State Government and factory management.

---

**(D) Role of the Certifying Surgeon (Section 10):**

The Certifying Surgeon is appointed by the State Government to:

1. **Conduct Medical Examinations** of young persons (adolescents) before employment.
2. **Issue Certificates of Fitness** valid for 12 months.
3. **Examine Suspended Workers** when their fitness is in doubt.
4. **Conduct Periodic Examinations** of workers in hazardous industries.
5. **Maintain Health Records** for at least 30 years (for hazardous processes).
6. **Certify** whether a worker is fit for night shifts, overtime, or hazardous work.

> The Certifying Surgeon ensures that **medical fitness** is verified before deployment, especially for young persons and hazardous process workers.

**Marks Allocation:**
- Safety provisions with sections: **4 marks**
- Health provisions with sections: **3 marks**
- Inspector's powers (Section 9): **3.5 marks**
- Certifying Surgeon's role (Section 10): **3.5 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing thresholds:** Students often write "100 workers for canteen" instead of **250**. Memorize: 30-C, 100-R, 150-FA, 250-C, 500-W, 1000-S.
>
> 2. **Overtime cap mistake:** Many students write "3 hours overtime" — the correct cap is **2 hours per day** (Section 64).
>
> 3. **Spread-over confusion:** Spread-over is **10.5 hours**, not 11 hours. It includes work + rest intervals combined.
>
> 4. **Child age error:** Don't confuse 14 vs. 15. **< 14 years = child (prohibited)**, 15–18 = adolescent (certificate required).
>
> 5. **Welfare Officer vs. Safety Officer:** Welfare Officer is for **500+ workers**, Safety Officer for **1000+ workers OR hazardous processes**.
>
> 6. **Overtime wages double, not 1.5x:** Section 59 mandates **2×** ordinary wages for overtime.
>
> 7. **Forget to mention Schedules:** The Act has **3 Schedules** — Schedule 1 (List of notifiable diseases), Schedule 2 (List of industries exempted for overtime), Schedule 3 (Special provisions for certain classes of dangerous operations).
>
> 8. **Omitting Penalty Sections:** Always mention Sections 92–106 when discussing penalties, not just "imprisonment."
>
> 9. **Citing only Section numbers, not Chapter numbers:** Examiners prefer chapter-level references (e.g., Chapter IV, not just Section 21).
>
> 10. **Confusing "Worker" with "Employee":** Worker under Factories Act is **specific to manufacturing processes**, not all employees.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — FACTORIES ACT, 1948:**

- **Year of Enactment:** 1948 (came into force 1949); replaced the 1934 Act.
- **Total Chapters:** 12 | **Sections:** 120 | **Schedules:** 3
- **Applicability Threshold:** **10 workers with power / 20 without power**.
- **Adult Age:** **18+ years** | Adolescent: **15–18 years** | Child: **< 14 years** (prohibited).
- **Working Hours:** Max **9/day**, **48/week**, spread-over **10.5/day**.
- **Rest Interval:** **30 minutes** after **5 hours** of continuous work.
- **Overtime Cap:** **2 hours/day**, **50 hours/quarter** at **double wages**.
- **Weekly Holiday:** **1 full day** (double wages if worked without compensatory off).
- **Annual Leave (Adult):** **1 day per 20 days worked**, max **30 days carry-forward**.
- **Annual Leave (Young):** **1 day per 15 days worked**, max **40 days carry-forward**.
- **Welfare Thresholds:**
  - Crèche: **30+ female workers** | Rest Room: **100+** | First Aid: 1 per **150**
  - Canteen: **250+** | Welfare Officer: **500+** | Safety Officer: **1000+** or hazardous.
- **Inspection:** By **Chief Inspector / Inspector** appointed by State Government.
- **Medical Examination:** By **Certifying Surgeon** for adolescents and hazardous workers.
- **Notice of Accident:** To Inspector within **prescribed time**.
- **Occupational Diseases:** **22 diseases** listed in Schedule 1, reportable under Section 89.
- **Child Labour Penalty:** Fine up to **₹50,000** or **2 years** imprisonment.
- **Hazardous Industries:** Regulated under 1989 Rules + 1987 Amendment; require **Safety Audit every 6 months**.
- **Penalties (General):** Fine up to **₹10,000** or **3 months** imprisonment.
- **Schedule 1 Diseases:** Lead poisoning, byssinosis, anthrax, asbestosis, silicosis, etc.
- **Schedule 2:** Industries exempted from overtime provisions.
- **Schedule 3:** Special provisions for **dangerous operations**.
- **Annual Return (Form 21)** and **Half-Yearly Return (Form 22)** must be filed.
- **Display Requirements:** Abstract of Act, working hours, holidays, emergency contacts.
- **Amendment Years:** 1976 (worker threshold), 1987 (hazardous processes), 2016 (women night shifts).
- **Practical HRM Application:** Records maintenance, safety committees, mock drills, POSH compliance coordination.
- **Remember the mantra:** "**Cleanliness, Care, Caution, Compliance**" — the 4 pillars of Factories Act.
- **One-liner for exams:** *"The Factories Act 1948 is the social contract between State, Capital, and Labour to ensure industrial democracy, safety, health, and welfare."*
